I.  INTRODUCTION 
Advances in big data and deep learning have promoted the 
wide adoption of artificial intelligence and machine lear ning 
(ML) in various fields [8, 27]. While ML models are embedded 
in app lications serving as face detection or voice recognition 
modules, the development and deployment of ML applications 
become more difficult and complex than traditional 
applications. For  example, the lifecycle of ML applications 
differs significantly from tr aditional applications in feedback 
loops [26], the monitoring and evaluation of a model may loop 
back to any previous step of development, such as data 
preprocessing or model training, which will change the model’s 
behavior. Besides, the massive datasets for model training and

back to any previous step of development, such as data 
preprocessing or model training, which will change the model’s 
behavior. Besides, the massive datasets for model training and 
testing also bring unignorable challenges to data storage and 
management [2, 11]. The differences and difficulties 
introduced by computational models have led to an increasing 
need for efficient and reliable production of ML applications as 
well as supporting infrastructures.  
Infrastructures and platforms for end -to-end lifecycle 
management of ML applications contain multiple parts of work 
due to the multiple proc ess phases and artifacts. T he lifecycle 
phases generally include several parts, such as data processing, 
data validation, feature extraction, model design, model 
training, model evaluation, application development, quality 
checks, application deployment, a nd application maintenance  
[4]. During these phases, datasets, models, application

training, model evaluation, application development, quality 
checks, application deployment, a nd application maintenance  
[4]. During these phases, datasets, models, application 
configurations, and other artifacts are generated [20]. Kinds of 
utilities are required for managing these phases and artifacts: 
ML platforms need to provide volu minous data  storage and 
even version control management for datasets and extracted 
features; in form of CPU, GPU, and TPU, considerable 
computing resources are demanded in the model training phase 
[23, 35, 36]; because well-trained models partly depend on and 
develop with datasets, managing and versioning models along 
with their datasets benefit system traceability, as what Data 
Version Control (DVC) does [21]; model performance decay 
happens over time for many reasons, such as changes in data

distribution of the envi ronment [9], and results in the 
requirement of model retraining mechanism; deployed 
monitoring and logging systems are needed for collecting 
model behavio r information that will feedback to previous 
development phases as a basis for model optimization, and  so 
on. Cloud shows its natural advantages in this domain [22, 32, 
41]. By offering bulk storage volumes and GPU-backed virtual 
machines (VMs), cloud providers like Amazon, Google, and 
Alibaba Cloud provide cloud storage and cloud computing 
services for ML  application developers through a pay -as-you-
go pricing model.  
Some platforms, such as TensorFlow Extended (TFX), 
ModelOps, and Kubeflow [4, 6,  20], have provided the 
solutions for the end -to-end lifecycle management of ML 
applications by orchestrating  its phases into multistep ML 
pipelines. Several key components and utilities are offered to 
manage data storage, access control, pipel ine execution, job

applications by orchestrating  its phases into multistep ML 
pipelines. Several key components and utilities are offered to 
manage data storage, access control, pipel ine execution, job 
scheduling, and performance monitoring. ModelOps and 
Kubeflow also provide pipeline configuration or Soft ware 
Development Kit (SDK) for developers to describe the ML 
workflows. These pipeline files may define several steps in 
corresponding with the lifecycle phases, such as data analyzing, 
model training, model evaluation, and model deployment. ML 
pipelines not only clarify the workflows but also benefit their 
automation by leveraging the concepts from DevOps [3]. The 
advantages of continuous integration (CI) and continuous 
delivery (CD) [13, 19], such as increasing developers’ 
productivity and quickly delivering codes, may also be applied 
to the iterative development and deployment  of ML 
applications. In particular, continuous training (CT) means

productivity and quickly delivering codes, may also be applied 
to the iterative development and deployment  of ML 
applications. In particular, continuous training (CT) means 
continuous training or retraining models when some specific 
events happen, such as the availability of new training data and 
model performance degradation [9]. Through applying DevOps 
principles to the lifecycle management of ML applications, 
concepts like MLOps are created to describe the combination 
of ML system development (Dev) and operation (Ops).  
Beyond ML code s, these frameworks and platforms have 
provided functional components and utilities to help avoid 
ongoing maintenance costs brought by hidden technical debt in 
ML systems [33]. With the aid of cloud and virtualization, the 
lifecycle management of ML applica tions benefits from ML 
pipeline platforms in efficiency and reliability. However, the 
performance of these platforms is still uncertain, such as how

lifecycle management of ML applica tions benefits from ML 
pipeline platforms in efficiency and reliability. However, the 
performance of these platforms is still uncertain, such as how 
much computing resources they consume and how much time it 
takes to train a model. Therefore, several experiments are set to 
evaluate this point by building ML pipelines and training 
models on Kubeflow with CI/CD tools [29]. Some metrics 
were used to conduct the consumption information collection 
and analysi s on seven deep learning neural networks with 
different network layers and parameters , such as GPU and 
memory utilization. The main contributions of this research are 
summarized as follows: 
• We verified the feasibility of building an effective ML 
pipeline with CI/CD abilities on machines with specific 
hardware configuration. 
• We evaluated the performance of ML pipeline 
platforms represented by Kubeflow, through a set of 
control experiments on se ven different models

hardware configuration. 
• We evaluated the performance of ML pipeline 
platforms represented by Kubeflow, through a set of 
control experiments on se ven different models 
according to GPU utilization and other metrics. 
• We explored the time and resource consumption 
concerning the ML platform and computational models. 
The rest of the paper is organized as follows: Section 2 
outlines some existing work that related to this study, Section 3 
introduces the method of evaluating ML platform performance 
taking Kubeflow as an ex ample, Section 4 details the 
experiment settings, including the platform composition, 
multistep ML pipeline construction, and metrics used, Section 
5 discusses and analyze s our experimental results, Section 7  
concludes our work. 
II. RELATED WORK 
As the number of software that employs computational 
models has surged, ML applications are evolving from ML 
programs into developmental ML systems. More than applying

II. RELATED WORK 
As the number of software that employs computational 
models has surged, ML applications are evolving from ML 
programs into developmental ML systems. More than applying 
software engineering principles to the lifecycle management of 
ML applications and clarifying the comp lexity of maintaining 
ML systems with different feedback loops, such a conceptual 
leap also brings out the challenges of providing vast and 
functional infrastructures a nd platforms that support the 
development and deployment of ML applications. 
A. ML Pipeline Platforms 
Developing and deploying ML applications is more than 
collecting data, training model s, and getting predictions; after 
all, connecting these part s while ignoring maintenance may 
lead to considerable technical debt  [33]. Towards efficient and 
reliable production of ML applications, many use cases and 
challenges need to be considered. For example, machine 
learning has been introduced into area s with high safety

reliable production of ML applications, many use cases and 
challenges need to be considered. For example, machine 
learning has been introduced into area s with high safety 
requirements, such as driverless technology and paramedical 
diagnostics, the quality and  privacy need to be assured before 
application serving, which requires testing and validation on 
both datasets and trained models. Building the work flow from 
data preprocessing to application runtime monitoring can be 
time-consuming and error -prone, automating this process can 
help users focus on the ML application development. Besides, 
when new training data arrives or model performance 
degradation is detected, the computational models need to be 
retrained and reserved, which requires effective feedback loops 
from the monitoring system to previous phases. 
ML platforms such as TFX [4] and ModelOps [20] address 
the abov e concerns. They provide end -to-end lifecycle

from the monitoring system to previous phases. 
ML platforms such as TFX [4] and ModelOps [20] address 
the abov e concerns. They provide end -to-end lifecycle 
management for ML applications and systems by offering a set 
of key components that are separate ly responsible for tasks 
such as data preprocessing, model training, model evaluation, 
and model serving. Based on  the pluggable and customizable 
components, these platforms intend to provide a generic 
solution for multiple development scenarios that  need to 
accomplish different ML tasks. Through configuring and 
linking these components, the ML workflows can be 
orchestrated into ML pipelines that are execution -supported on 
these platforms. While there is a trend of training ML models 
on the cloud with abund ant GPU-backed VMs and containers, 
these platforms are able to carry out ML pipelines in hybrid 
environments. Besides the production -level reliability and

scalability, continuous training abilities are also provided by 
these two platforms to adapt to evolv ing da ta or increase 
change frequency.  
B. MLOps 
The ML applications and systems differ from traditional 
software in many ways w hich makes it necessary to ascertain 
custom DevOps for ML features. Compared to conv entional 
software systems, ML systems include  additional model 
artifacts, contain more data process steps, and deal with more 
complex relationships between these three ar tifacts. Due to the 
introduction of computational models along with their 
experimental nature, traceability and reproducibility of the 
training results are required [31], which can benefit from 
versioning models together with  codes and datasets. Used as 
training and testing datasets, the higher quality requirement of 
data in ML systems need s m ore data process steps in ML 
workflows, such as data cleaning, data analyzing, data 
validation, feature extraction, and so on. The perfor mance

data in ML systems need s m ore data process steps in ML 
workflows, such as data cleaning, data analyzing, data 
validation, feature extraction, and so on. The perfor mance 
monitoring feedback loops and new data availability may lead 
to the optimizations of codes and models, which bring out new 
versions of these three artifacts in the iterations of ML systems.  
MLOps is an engineering practice in the ML field that 
applies DevOps principles to ML systems for unifying the ML 
system development and operation [9]. From the CI perspective, 
more test procedures are introduced in addition to classical unit 
and integration tests, such as data and model validation s. From 
the CD perspective, processed datasets and trained models are 
delivered from data and deep learning scientists to ML system 
engineers automatic ally and continuously. From the CT 
perspective, the arrival of new data and the degradation of 
model performance require  to trigger model retraining  or

engineers automatic ally and continuously. From the CT 
perspective, the arrival of new data and the degradation of 
model performance require  to trigger model retraining  or 
improve model performance through online methods [40]. 
C. Kubeflow 
Kubernetes is an open -source cluster manager to arrange 
Docker containers among a mass of virtual or physical 
machines, which is increasingly adopted in cloud computing [5, 
16, 28]. Besides creating, monitoring and scheduling containers 
to execute programs and run software, Ku bernetes also 
supports the ability to build custom resources (CRs) according 
to declarative specifications. With the increasing usage in more 
and more AI/ML companies and groups, challenges of 
developing and deploying ML applications in Kubernetes 
clusters have emerged. For example, various components and 
mixing vendors are involved in building ML systems, which 
introduces many sophisticated configurations to connecting and

clusters have emerged. For example, various components and 
mixing vendors are involved in building ML systems, which 
introduces many sophisticated configurations to connecting and 
managing these services [10]. In many cases, these 
configurations are bound to parti cular c lusters, which may 
impair the mobility of moving ML applications from local 
clusters to the cloud or from development environments to 
production environments.  
Based on Kubernetes, Kubeflow is an open -source 
pipelined ML platform created to address the above concerns. 
It provides end -to-end lifecycle management of ML 
applications by taking advantage of TFX components, such as 
data validation, model evaluation, and model serving. Because 
of the ability to be deployed in kinds of Kubernetes clusters, 
Kubeflow enables ML workflows to work in any local or cloud 
cluster where Kubeflow has b een installed, which benefits the 
simplification of setups and portability on diverse

Kubeflow enables ML workflows to work in any local or cloud 
cluster where Kubeflow has b een installed, which benefits the 
simplification of setups and portability on diverse 
infrastructures. Beyond that, a TensorFlow CR is defined to use 
CPUs or GPUs and adjusted to the size of a cluster [1, 10]. 
Kubeflow Pipelines is the component in Kubeflow that 
manages and orchestrates the end-to-end ML workflows. Steps 
in an ML workflow are wrapped as components in Kubeflow 
Pipelines, such as data preprocessing, data tr ansformation, and 
model training. After being packaged as Docker images, these 
components are reusable across pipelines in a containerized 
way. Consisting of a set of components as well as their 
input/output relationships, the pipelines are defined in Pyth on 
files through the SDK, which will be compiled into static 
YAML-format configurations for Kubeflow to process [25]. 
These pipeline files can be packaged and uploaded to the

files through the SDK, which will be compiled into static 
YAML-format configurations for Kubeflow to process [25]. 
These pipeline files can be packaged and uploaded to the 
Kubeflow platform in CI/CD procedures when the files change 
in the code repositor y, while the running o f pipelines can be 
triggered on schedule or through the message sent by cloud 
storage when new data arrives. 
III. METHOD 
To explore and estimate the performance of ML pipelines 
on a specific platform, we need to build an ML platform from 
existing work first. Meanwhile, we want  the platform to have 
continuous training ability in the form of retraining models 
automatically when specific  events happen in a relatively 
frequent way, such as ML algorithm code s change. Finally, we 
construct the whole effective ML pipeline that can be executed 
on this platform.

B. Pipeline Construction 
After Kubeflow, Gitea and Drone have been deployed and 
configured to connect  each other, we need to design and 
construct our ML pipeline  that can continuously train models 
when specific events happen, such as code change submissions 
in our case. From this pe rspective, t he ML pipeline  can be 
divided into two functional parts, one is the completion of ML 
tasks and the other is the compilation of ML task definitions. 
As shown in Figure 1, we set up two kinds of pipelines on 
the ML platform: the CI/CD pipeline  used for packaging and 
uploading Kubeflow pipelines automatically, and the Kubeflow 
pipeline used for model training and other processes.  
In the CI/CD pipeline, upon receipt of the messages that 
represent git repository c hanges, the CI/CD tool first needs t o

pipeline used for model training and other processes.  
In the CI/CD pipeline, upon receipt of the messages that 
represent git repository c hanges, the CI/CD tool first needs t o 
clone repositories that store the codes of computational models 
and Kubeflow pipeline definition files. Second, based on the 
Dockerfiles in repositories, Docker images that contain pipeline 
component pro grams are built and uploaded to the Docker 
repository. Then, referencing these components and defining 
their in -out interactions, the pipeline files are compiled into 
YAML-format configurations and uploaded to Kubeflow. 
Finally, the CI/CD tool starts the e xecution of Kubeflow 
pipelines. These above steps are described in the CI/CD 
pipeline configuration files stored in git repositories as well. 
Kubeflow pipelines are carried out by sequentially 
executing components. During the execution of a component, 
the Docker image  is pulled from the Docker repository f irst.

IV. EXPERIMENT SETUP 
This section elaborates on the  runtime environment of our 
ML platform and the experiments set to train different models 
in ML pipelines whose time and resource consumption  were 
collected according to some metrics. 
A. Runtime Environment 
Our platform was built on a physical machine with an  Intel 
I7-8700K CPU, a GeForce GTX 1080 graphics card, 32GB 
memory, and 8GB video memory. We first deployed a single -
node local Kubernetes cluster on this machine and then 
installed Kubeflow and Kubeflow Pipeline in it. We deployed 
Gitea, Drone , Docker , and Docker repository in the same

node local Kubernetes cluster on this machine and then 
installed Kubeflow and Kubeflow Pipeline in it. We deployed 
Gitea, Drone , Docker , and Docker repository in the same 
machine. We also configured the ML algorithm programs in 
the containers to use the GPU to train models. 
B. Models and Metrics 
Model-involved processes are the most important parts of 
ML pipelines. To explore the time and resour ce consumption 
of the ML platform during pipeline execution s, we selected 
several deep learning neural networks with different layers and 
parameters, packaged them into Docker images, and took them 
as model training and model testing components in differen t 
Kubeflow pipelines. 
Table 1 lists the selected deep learning neural networks 
trained in our ML pipeline experiments: MobileNet [17], 
ShuffleNet [42], ResNet [15], VG G [34], DenseNet [18], 
GoogLeNet [37] and Inception V3 [38]. These are approved

networks that have a wide range  of layers ranging from 16 to 
121 and hyperparameters ranging from 1.0 to 34.0 million, 
whose implementations are sourced from the Github 
community [39]. With epoch set to 200, these models were 
trained on CIFAR -100 [24], a classic da taset that is 
approximately 161 MB in size.  
Different ML pipelines were constructed to train different 
models. Finally, we ran each pipeline 7 times on our ML 
platform fro m code submission  to model testing. To explore 
the performance of different steps in  the ML pipeline, the time 
and resource consumption during executions were collected by 
step. We respectively recorded the time consumption for the 
CI/CD pipeline  and Kubeflow pipeline, in which  the time 
elapsed of git rep ository cloning and other substeps  in the 
CI/CD phase were separately recorded too. As for resources, 
we mainly recorded the consumption during Kubeflow pipeline 
executions, according to some metr ics from Nvidia

CI/CD phase were separately recorded too. As for resources, 
we mainly recorded the consumption during Kubeflow pipeline 
executions, according to some metr ics from Nvidia 
documentation, such as GPU utilization, memo ry utilization, 
and framebuffer memory used [30].

B. Resource Consumption 
The computing resources are mainly need ed in ML task 
accomplishing stages such as model training, so we recorded 
the utilization inform ation during the executions of Kubeflow

pipelines. As shown in Figure 3, the average GPU utilization 
rate is all between 89.756 % and 98.3 % with little distinc tion, 
even when training different models that vary greatly in layers 
from 16 to 121 and in parameters from 1 to 34  million. While 
memory utilization  rate and framebuffer (FB) memory used 
vary with the computational models, from 47.424% to 72.554% 
and 963.245 to 6795.425 MiB respectively, and are both 
relatively low compared to GPU utilization. High uti lization 
not only means  being able to make full use of GPU  but also 
means that programs will compete for GPU usage when 
executing multiple model training t asks simultaneously. This 
also brings a problem for ML platform providers, which is how 
to improve memory and framebuffer memory utilization when 
GPU usage reaches a bottleneck.

VI. CONCLUSION  
In this study, we deployed an ML platform with DevOps 
capability on a gen erally configured physical machine, which 
verified the feasibility of building functional ML pipelines 
from existing CI/CD tools and ML platforms such as Kubeflow, 
which can continuously retrain models when specific events 
happen. The whole ML pipeline can  be finely divided into ML

from existing CI/CD tools and ML platforms such as Kubeflow, 
which can continuously retrain models when specific events 
happen. The whole ML pipeline can  be finely divided into ML 
task pipeline that carries out ML tasks including data 
preprocessing and model training, and CI/CD  pipeline that 
continuously tri ggers executions of ML task pipelines and 
deploys validated models. On the platform represented by 
Kubeflow, most of the time and resource consumption is 
generated by the ML task pipeline. The executions of CI/CD 
pipelines ca n be completed in seconds, with  the most time 
spent compiling Kubeflow pipeline definition files into the 
platform-supported format . According to some metrics, the 
GPU utilization during the ML task pipeline executions is 
generally high though the models traine d vary greatly in the 
number of  layers and parameters, which may cause 
performance bottlenecks when training multiple models

generally high though the models traine d vary greatly in the 
number of  layers and parameters, which may cause 
performance bottlenecks when training multiple models 
simultaneously. In addition, the time and resource consumption 
have more to do with the design and construction of models 
than ML platforms.

Abstract - Machine Learning is a widely popular field that is being 
used in an increasingly large number of projects worldwide. This 
necessitates the use of certain practices to create a structured 
framework for such projects. These practices, processes and 
pipelines are termed as Machine Learning Operations (MLOps). 
The software development lifecycle for a machine learning is 
indeed very complex and sequential nature of it results in several 
repetitive tasks for developers. Automation in this aspect would 
greatly red uce time and manual effort required. Jenkins is an 
open-source continuous integration tool that can be used to build

repetitive tasks for developers. Automation in this aspect would 
greatly red uce time and manual effort required. Jenkins is an 
open-source continuous integration tool that can be used to build 
pipelines to define and automate workflows in MLOps domain. 
This paper proposes the design and implementation of pipelines 
for various stag es of MLOps that are data analysis, data 
preparation, training, testing and deployment on a single 
platform. 
 
Keywords—Machine Learning Operations (MLOps), Software 
Development Lifecycle (SDLC), Machine Learning (ML), 
Continuous Integration (CI), Source Code Management (SCM). 
I. INTRODUCTION  
The SDLC of a project defines certain practices which allow a 
project to be structured and easy to debug in case of errors or 
faults, even though there may be several stakeholders and 
developers working on it. SDLC has been a widely used 
framework for several decades now, and several SDLC standards

faults, even though there may be several stakeholders and 
developers working on it. SDLC has been a widely used 
framework for several decades now, and several SDLC standards 
have been set by regulatory authorities. However, since advent 
of Machine Learning in past few decades, several organizations 
and projects have started to implement Machine Learning to suit 
their use cases. When it comes to production, managing ML 
projects has been a difficult affair for several years now . During 
this time data scientists, analysts, developers and other 
stakeholders of an ML project have researched on ways to 
collaborate and work together . This collaboration has many 
uncertainties and complexities. Hence, it is not easy to overcome 
them. This is one of the major reasons why most ML projects do 
not reach the production phase at all . In recent past, several 
principles are being standardized, and tool s being proposed to

them. This is one of the major reasons why most ML projects do 
not reach the production phase at all . In recent past, several 
principles are being standardized, and tool s being proposed to 
allow teams to collaborate on production -level ML projects .  
Development of these tools had multiple targets , preprocessing 
of data, model building, training the model, testing, deployment, 
and monitoring are some of them. As the field of AI progresses 
such kind of tools are constantly emerging. 
MLOps involves several sequential processes such as data 
preprocessing, model training, model testing and model 
deployment. Throughout the lifecycle of an ML project, multiple 
changes are made to each of these stages. However,  
 
 
 
after the changes are made,  need to build and run these stages 
from beginning, in a sequential order to makes it a repetitive and 
time-consuming process. Moreover, MLOps is a relatively new

after the changes are made,  need to build and run these stages 
from beginning, in a sequential order to makes it a repetitive and 
time-consuming process. Moreover, MLOps is a relatively new 
field, and standards are still being discussed and researched over.  
In this paper, Jenkins, a CI tool is used to automate various stages 
in MLOps. Jenkins uses pipelines which allow developers to 
define a structured workflow for sequential stages. A pipeline is 
set of jobs that are se quential or linked to one another. Using 
plugins, Jenkins implements CICD pipelines. Jenkins pipeline 
has several advantages. first of them being that the pipeline can 
easily be edited and changed by several users, which is especially 
useful in projects in volving large teams. Secondly, even if 
system that the pipeline is running on loses power or switches 
off, the pipeline resumes the progress from where it left off. Most 
important of all, pipelines can be made to run parallel on multiple

off, the pipeline resumes the progress from where it left off. Most 
important of all, pipelines can be made to run parallel on multiple 
systems simultaneously, hence speeding up processes like model 
preprocessing and model training. Through this automation, if 
developer makes a change in the code for the data preprocessing 
stage, Jenkins CI would automatically build and run all the 
forthcoming stages withou t the need for manual intervention. 
This would greatly reduce time, manual effort and cost for ML 
projects. This method sequentially implements all the stages of 
the machine learning process, for changes made on a Source 
Code Management tool such as Git. An email notification is also 
sent to the engineer after completion of each stage, with 
information on the various results of the stage such as accuracy 
and error rate, and the error logs in case of faults in the program.

Integration and Continuous Delivery (CICD ) pipeline 
increases the efficiency of large -scale projects. Timeline of a 
project and its delivery is of utmost importance to companies 
and to ensure that the client delivery date is met, a CICD 
pipeline becomes very vital. The test phase in CICD pipeline s 
can sometimes not simulate the production performance 
behaviour as they cannot model the exact traffic in the real -
world scenario. To solve this problem, the paper proposes a 
three-step automation phase with benchmark, load test and 
scaling. The paper wa s implemented using the Jenkins CI 
server. GoReplay was then used to simulate the traffic from the 
production environment point of view.  Nikita Seth et al. [4] 
implemented Jenkins as a tool for software patch integration

III. DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION METHODOLOGY  
 
Fig.1.  MLOps Cycle 
MLOps is a cyclic process. Figure 1 illustrates the major steps 
in this process. Exploratory Data Analysis or EDA is the step 
in which the problem statement is analyzed, and the data is 
explored for the ML lifecycle through datasets and 
visualizations. Data preparation involves the transformation, 
aggregation and sometimes omission of unnecessary data

explored for the ML lifecycle through datasets and 
visualizations. Data preparation involves the transformation, 
aggregation and sometimes omission of unnecessary data 
through methods such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) 
and Feature Subset Selection to make sure that the data is fit to 
be used to train the model. 
Then the model is trained and tuned with the help of several 
ML libraries such as scikit -learn and optuna to improve the 
hyperparameters. Next, the model reviewed by analyzing the 
history of changes to the model and the several versions of the 
model. Model inference includes the process of analyzing the 
results from the model and measuring the accuracy to verify 
that the model can be deployed onto the production level 
environment. Then the model is deployed onto a server or 
cloud-based environment through applications such as Docker. 
This process repeats and the model is retrained iteratively until 
the desired results are achieved.  Methodology and

cloud-based environment through applications such as Docker.

the desired results are achieved.  Methodology and

• The Dockerfile is defined with all the necessary

• A container contains everything necessary to run the

It must be noted that this paper uses Docker, because it is a good 
practice in software development to use containerized 
applications. This is done to ensure that the application works

It must be noted that this paper uses Docker, because it is a good 
practice in software development to use containerized 
applications. This is done to ensure that the application works 
similarly when run on any machine in the production 
environment since the environment variables and dependencies 
required by the application are present in the container. 
The process of creation of Dockerfile is quite simple. The 
Dockerfile can be seen as a raw text file. It contains the following 
instructions: 
• FROM – This is the first entry in any Dockerfile and 
defines the parent image.  
• ARG – Once the Docker image is run, the variables that 
are defined in this section are used. 
• CMD – Upon the creation of the Docker container this 
command is executed. Only one CMD instruction can 
be used per Dockerfile 
• LABEL – It includes information about the docker file 
in key-value pairs such as the author’s name and version 
details.

be used per Dockerfile 
• LABEL – It includes information about the docker file 
in key-value pairs such as the author’s name and version 
details.   
• RUN – These are the instructions to be executed when 
the image is built. 
• USER – This defines the username of the user that 
created the Dockerfile.  
• ADD – This is used to mo ve files from a source to the 
required destination.  
• EXPOSE – During runtime, Docker listens to the port 
mentioned in this instruction.  
• ENV – Used to set the environment variables so that 
they are similar on both the source system and the 
production environment.  
• WORKDIR – The current directory on which the 
Dockerfile must be executed

considers three different containers to execute

Figure 3 illustrates the block diagram implementation. For this 
example, the real estate price prediction is chosen. The paper 
considers three different containers to execute 
preprocessing.py, train.py and test.py.  The preprocessing.py 
file receives the CSV file of the data as input and splits it into 
the training and test sets accordingly. The trai n and test CSV 
files are then passed on to the next stages as input. The train.py 
file then uses the training data set to train the model and outputs 
the trained model’s Joblib file and the meta data from the logs 
of the training. 
The test.py file receives the test data and the Joblib file as input 
and stores the output in a test metadata file. These three stages 
are implemented on Jenkins to automate the entire process of 
ML projects. Several times, there are changes that are required 
to be made in the dat aset. In that case, the user can use the 
Jenkins pipeline to automatically go through all these three

ML projects. Several times, there are changes that are required 
to be made in the dat aset. In that case, the user can use the 
Jenkins pipeline to automatically go through all these three 
stages immediately after the change in the data.csv file is 
committed on Git or a similar SCM tool. If a change is made in 
the preprocessing, training or the testing phases, the next stages 
can be automatically run, accordingly.  Since ML projects 
usually involve several changes to both the data and the model 
parameters, several times throughout the project, to obtain 
better accuracies, this process of autom ating the stages uses 
Jenkins saves time and effort.  The software requirements of 
this implementation include a minimum of 1GB of RAM and 
10GB of Storage for the Docker container to run. It also 
requires the installation of the latest version of JDK (Java 
Development Kit), and a web browser such as Google Chrome.

VI. RESULTS AND ANALYSIS 
 
Fig.6. Successful Pipeline run 
The Jenkins Pipeline was successfully able to run through the 
three major stages of the project and helped automate the 
redundant tasks after each change in the data or the source 
code. For an ML project without this automation, the user 
would have to check the status of each stage before running the 
next. The entire process of monitoring and executing will have 
to be done after any change. However, this implementation 
automatically runs the stages once a change is made on the 
SCM tool.  
After the completion  of the stages, or during any error in one 
of the stages, the error logs or success report is sent via Mail, 
with the help of the SMTP plugin in Jenkins.

with the standard accuracy on this dataset. This shows that the 
automated MLOps pipeline was able to achieve the accuracy 
generated by manual tweaking of the model, in much lesser

with the standard accuracy on this dataset. This shows that the

VII. IMPLEMENTATION CHALLENGES IN MLOPS 
Though MLOps is a promising field, i t does pose several 
challenges. Firstly, unlike other software projects, a small 
change in the data almost always has a major effect on the 
output of the model. Hence any ML project is an iterative 
process. This requires very quick response times, from cod e 
integration to deployment.  
Also, there are a multitude of tools and people involved in 
every ML project. This includes analysts, business executives 
and IT personnel. The pipeline, therefore, must be highly 
customizable, and easy to handle. 
The lack of implementation of SDLC standards in MLOps is 
another major hurdle. MLOps is a novel field and hence lacks 
sufficient research and literature. The implementation

The lack of implementation of SDLC standards in MLOps is 
another major hurdle. MLOps is a novel field and hence lacks 
sufficient research and literature. The implementation 
presented in this paper proposes to solve all the challenges. 
The limitations of existing metho ds for MLOps are that they 
are not completely automated or only a few steps such as 
preprocessing are partially automated. This leads to several 
redundant tasks being done manually especially in the training 
and testing stages of the ML project. 
VIII. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE SCOPE 
A. Conclusion  
From the obtained results it can be observed that the 
automation of three major stages of ML project reduces manual 
effort required to run training and testing stages, every time a 
change is done to the data or preprocessing steps. Since 
industries and ML is moving towards data driven algorithms 
and technologies, this type of sequential automation will 
reduce manual time by automating most of the redundant tasks

industries and ML is moving towards data driven algorithms 
and technologies, this type of sequential automation will 
reduce manual time by automating most of the redundant tasks 
in the process of ML. The same Jenkins pipeline can  be used 
for several projects with very few modifications. This leads to 
all projects in an organization to come under one umbrella, 
making it easier for engineers, analysts and business executives 
to better understand the project and work in collaboration. Use 
of an automated Jenkins pipeline is a more hassle -free and 
faster experience for ML projects than manual monitoring and 
execution. This is extremely useful in cases of teams working 
on projects, where a stable change made on SCM tool can 
immediately run stages and deploy updated model to cloud 
services for the end -user to access immediately.  This 
implementation also greatly reduces time between code base 
changes and deployment of the project.

Abstract—DevOps practices are the de facto sandard when
developing software. The increased adoption of machine learning
(ML) to solve problems urges us to adapt all the current
approaches to developing a new standard that can take full
benefit from the new solution. In this work we propose a
graphical representation for DevOps for ML-based applications,
namely MLOps, and also outline open research challenges. The
pipeline aims to get the best of both worlds by maintaining
the simple and iconic pipeline of DevOps, yet improving it by
adding new circular steps for ML incorporation. This aims to
create an ML-based development subsystem that can be self-

the simple and iconic pipeline of DevOps, yet improving it by
adding new circular steps for ML incorporation. This aims to
create an ML-based development subsystem that can be self-
maintained, and is capable of evolving side-by-side with the
software development.
Index Terms—MLOps, AIOps, Software Engineering, DevOps
I. I NTRODUCTION
Companies are getting more and more interested in ap-
plying DevOps principles for Machine Learning (ML) based
software. DevOps brings people, processes, and tools together
to produce continuous values at high velocity [1], connecting
Development and Operations as a continuous pipeline.
Developing agile software based on ML requires different
steps compared to normal DevOps. The incorporation process
of an ML pipeline when developing software needs to be
carefully addressed so that the ML-based software system can
be assured of long-term maintainability and evolvability.
The goal of this paper is to define a clear pipeline when

carefully addressed so that the ML-based software system can
be assured of long-term maintainability and evolvability.
The goal of this paper is to define a clear pipeline when
practicing DevOps to ML-based software, namely MLOps.
Such a pipeline will provide essential guidelines when de-
veloping ML-based software starting from DevOps.
Therefore, we propose an extension of the DevOps pipeline,
adapted to MLOps. In this work we aim at highlighting
the diversification yet affinity when developing both software
and ML procedures essential for the creation of ML-based
software, providing a more comprehensive list of steps, dif-
ferentiating the steps required for the development of the ML-
specific code and the whole system development. Our work
differs from [2] in that we aim to give a high level overview
of the process.
Multiple works faced the problem of defining a new pipeline
capable of describing the process in a simple yet complete

differs from [2] in that we aim to give a high level overview
of the process.
Multiple works faced the problem of defining a new pipeline
capable of describing the process in a simple yet complete
way. Zhou et al. [3] verified the feasibility of building an
ML pipeline with CI/CD capabilities. In [4] and [5] multiple
challenges have been addressed when adopting MLOps. Such
challenges have been categorized in context and data and
educating for AI operations. John et al. [6] conducted a
literature review to present the state of the adoption of MLOps
in practice to derive and validate a framework for the identi-
fication of activity involved when adopting MLOps. M ¨akinen
et al. [7] performed a survey with the goal of understanding to
what extent MLOps is needed in today’s ML operations. The
result of the survey showed that as we are moving towards
ecosystems where teams of developers are working closely in
ML development, there is an urge to define a clear MLOps

result of the survey showed that as we are moving towards
ecosystems where teams of developers are working closely in
ML development, there is an urge to define a clear MLOps
pipeline. The reasons behind this are the inability to simply
apply plug-and-play DevOps tools and principles, and the lack
of a definition of a full stack ML pipeline. Colantoni et al. [8]
defined a theoretical model to integrate ML into DevOps,
however the model does not propose a visualization of the
whole pipeline, nor a complete integration of the ML steps.
Researchers investigated different aspects of MLOps, but
did not provide a clear picture of the different processes
adopted for the ML and for the non-ML software processes.
In the remainder of this paper, we present our proposal of
MLOps representation, as well as open research challenges
towards a joint research plan on MLOps practices.
II. F ROM DEVOPS TO MLO PS
The adoption of DevOps practices in software development

MLOps representation, as well as open research challenges
towards a joint research plan on MLOps practices.
II. F ROM DEVOPS TO MLO PS
The adoption of DevOps practices in software development
has become a requirement in most scenarios nowadays. The
reason behind this is not only related to the improved results
achieved, but also the culture created by the adoption of these
practices. In a world where the new mantra is be faster, be
more agile, DevOps fits perfectly in, due to its dynamic nature
based on continuous learning and improving.
The infinite DevOps loop aims to portray the role division
of application Developers (Dev) and the IT Operations (Ops)
teams in a singular team. The Dev is responsible for planning,
coding, building and testing while the release, deploy and
monitor are the tasks of the Ops. The rapid adoption of ML-
based software created a new figure: the ML developer. Such
figures exist and operate in parallel with the software developer

monitor are the tasks of the Ops. The rapid adoption of ML-
based software created a new figure: the ML developer. Such
figures exist and operate in parallel with the software developer
and therefore needs to be embedded in the DevOps pipeline.

The P LANNING phase covers everything that happens be-
fore the developers start writing code, including requirement
gathering and architectural design. While considering ML,
the planning refers to the identification of the problem to
be solved and the available data. Based on these, the most
appropriate data analysis approaches can be selected (e.g.,
classification or regression, supervised or unsupervised), and
suitable algorithms can be selected (e.g. Random Forest,
Deep Learning). In the case of supervised algorithms, a data
labelling step might be also needed.
In the CODING phase, both the system and the ML code
need to be implemented and then locally validated.
The VALIDATION , in the traditional DevOps, is usually
performed before committing the code, running tests locally.

The VALIDATION , in the traditional DevOps, is usually

The ML validation refers to the evaluation of the performance
of the ML model, with data not previously encountered. If the
validation proves that the ML-based approach is not suitable
for the data or for the algorithms, it is necessary to return
to the planning step to optimize the model to better tackle
the problem. Once the ML optimizations and local testing of
the system have been performed, the ML code needs to be
integrated into the system code.
The system B UILDING and T ESTING phases are then per-
formed as in traditional DevOps.
IV. O PEN RESEARCH CHALLENGES
Our roadmap, towards an integrated MLOps process, in-
cludes several challenges that might be investigated within the
software engineering and the ML community. We started our
investigation [10] [5], highlighting the main software quality

cludes several challenges that might be investigated within the
software engineering and the ML community. We started our
investigation [10] [5], highlighting the main software quality
issues of AI systems. In the future we aim at:
• Understand how MLOps is adopted in companies.
• Delineate what kind of problems MLOps practices may
be best suited for.
• Define MLOps by adapting the concepts, methods and
tools used in DevOps.
• Explore other fields of research where MLOps practices
can be performed (e.g. CVOps [11]).
V. C ONCLUSIONS
The increased adoption of ML-based software generated
a demand for ML developers who need to perform tasks in
parallel to software developers.
In this paper we propose a better representation for MLOps,
including ML development steps into the traditional DevOps
practices. The MLOps pipeline focuses on the dualism be-
tween software and ML developers and their tasks. Compared
to normal DevOps we have a new block (validation) and those

practices. The MLOps pipeline focuses on the dualism be-
tween software and ML developers and their tasks. Compared
to normal DevOps we have a new block (validation) and those
related to plan and code are now field-specific. Moreover, the
validation blocks produce two extra loops (optimization and
debug) for the ML side and one for the software side (debug).
This work is part of a roadmap for the development of
MLOps practices that will be investigated collaboratively with
the ML and software engineering communities.

Abstract—Machine Learning Operations (MLOps), derived
from DevOps, aims to unify the development, deployment, and
maintenance of machine learning (ML) models. Continuous
training (CT) automatically retrains ML models, and continuous
deployment (CD) automatically deploys the retrained models to
production. CT and CD are essential for maintaining ML model
performance in dynamic production environments and, therefore,
need to be considered when practicing MLOps. We present our
CTCD-e MLOps pipeline, implemented mostly using existing
open-source software, being able to autonomously adapt an ML
system to changing production environments by enabling ﬂexible

CTCD-e MLOps pipeline, implemented mostly using existing
open-source software, being able to autonomously adapt an ML
system to changing production environments by enabling ﬂexible
model CT and CD. The pipeline can automatically trigger a
model retraining round when the model performance degrades.
Then it automatically conducts an A/B test for the retrained
model and its predecessor in production to start serving the
better one. The pipeline was evaluated by two experiments. In
the pipeline, users can ﬂexibly conﬁgure the model retraining, as
well as the redeployment and production A/B test of the retrained
models based on various requirements.
Index Terms—MLOps, continuous training, continuous deploy-
ment, A/B testing, open-source, experimentation, pipeline
I. I NTRODUCTION
Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) [1], derived from
DevOps, is a collection of practices and techniques to unify the
development, deployment, and operation of machine learning

I. I NTRODUCTION
Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) [1], derived from
DevOps, is a collection of practices and techniques to unify the
development, deployment, and operation of machine learning
(ML) systems, aiming to streamline the process of shifting ML
solutions to production. MLOps covers multiple phases of an
ML project, such as data engineering, model training and eval-
uation, model validation, model deployment and serving, and
model monitoring. An MLOps pipeline brings tool automation
into MLOps [2] by multiple pipeline components responsible
for different MLOps phases. As the production environment
inevitably changes or cannot be covered comprehensively,
it signiﬁcantly affects ML models’ performance as they are
sensitive to dynamic data – models which have passed initial
validation tests may not perform as expected in the long
run. For example, a recommender system may fail to provide
accurate recommendations due to the new items it has not

validation tests may not perform as expected in the long
run. For example, a recommender system may fail to provide
accurate recommendations due to the new items it has not
been trained on before or because the customers’ preferences
have changed. Therefore, an ML model needs retraining to
maintain its performance.
Especially when changes are frequent or uncertainty is high,
continuous training (CT) and continuous deployment (CD)
We acknowledge the IML4E project of ITEA funded by Business Finland.
are needed for ML models. CT enables automatic model
retraining, and CD automatically deploys retrained models to
production. They enable ML systems to respond to changes in
production by keeping models fresh. Retraining can be trig-
gered periodically or by model monitoring results or repository
updates [3]. A commonly used strategy during CD in today’s
software engineering is A/B testing , meaning experimenting
with a redeployed model on a small percentage of user trafﬁc.

updates [3]. A commonly used strategy during CD in today’s
software engineering is A/B testing , meaning experimenting
with a redeployed model on a small percentage of user trafﬁc.
A/B testing can validate the performance of a retrained model
in production and mitigate the risk of deploying a poorly
performing model, further elevating the effectiveness of model
CT and CD.
Proprietary and open-source solutions are available for
developing MLOps pipelines with CT and CD support for
models. However, proprietary solutions suffer from a lack of
transparency and potential vendor lock-in, while existing open-
source solutions may not offer ﬂexible model retraining capa-
bilities as elaborated on in SectionII. Due to these limitations,
we identiﬁed an opportunity for a solution, implementable
by open-source software, which provides ML models with
ﬂexible CT and CD support.
This paper presents an open-source and portable MLOps
pipeline called CTCD-e (continuous-training-and-continuous-

by open-source software, which provides ML models with
ﬂexible CT and CD support.
This paper presents an open-source and portable MLOps
pipeline called CTCD-e (continuous-training-and-continuous-
deployment-enabling), a practical solution to autonomously
adapt ML systems to changing data by providing ﬂexible CT
and CD support for models. We designed a conceptual model
of our MLOps pipeline and implemented it utilizing open-
source tools as far as reasonable 1. The pipeline can automat-
ically start to retrain a model when its performance degrades
and automatically A/B test the retrained model against its
predecessor in production. The pipeline was evaluated by two
experiments, which also highlight future research directions in
CT/CD-aware MLOps pipelines.
This paper is structured as follows. The previous work
is in Section II. We present the conceptual model of the
CTCD-e MLOps pipeline in Section III and its implemen-
tation walkthrough in Section IV. We describe the evaluation

is in Section II. We present the conceptual model of the
CTCD-e MLOps pipeline in Section III and its implemen-
tation walkthrough in Section IV. We describe the evaluation
in Section V. We discuss our MLOps pipeline and further

II. P REVIOUS WORK
CT and CD have been considered essential operations for
improving the automation level and, thereby, the maturity
of MLOps. Google [4] presents a framework for accessing
MLOps maturity. Level-0 MLOps has no automation, and
model development and deployment are manual. In level-1
MLOps, a pipeline is established to bring automation, with
model CT and CD being the important objectives of the
pipeline. Level-2 MLOps, taking a further step from level-
1, allows updating the pipeline components frequently. Two
MLOps frameworks have been derived from literature reviews
[5], [6], both of which recognize model CT and CD as
necessary activities for adapting models to dynamic production
environments. A literature review [3] outlines the retraining
triggers used for model retraining and discusses the challenges
of continuously deploying models to production. A reference
architecture [7] has been proposed to retrain models using

triggers used for model retraining and discusses the challenges
of continuously deploying models to production. A reference
architecture [7] has been proposed to retrain models using
the new incoming data, and an MLOps framework called
“CD4ML” (Continuous Delivery for Machine Learning) [8]
has been introduced to trigger model retraining rounds based
on the information collected from model monitoring. These
two are high-level guidelines for implementing an MLOps
pipeline with model CT and CD functionalities but lack
concrete implementations. In an example implementation2
for the CD4ML framework, the ﬂow is single-directional, from
model training to model monitoring, without retraining triggers
from the model monitoring.
Proprietary pipelines exist with CT and CD support, espe-
cially from cloud vendors. For example, AWS SageMaker [9]
enables users to build MLOps pipelines where data drift can
trigger model retraining. Another example proprietary MLOps

cially from cloud vendors. For example, AWS SageMaker [9]
enables users to build MLOps pipelines where data drift can
trigger model retraining. Another example proprietary MLOps
pipeline has been implemented to support deploying models
to edge devices [10], where performance degradation triggers
model retraining. The implementation relies on Azure services.
However, proprietary pipelines have limitations, such as a lack
of transparency and potential vendor lock-in.
Much effort has been dedicated to developing MLOps
pipelines using and integrating the existing open-source tools
designed for one or more speciﬁc activities or tasks of MLOps
[11], [12]. However, the focus is on model training and deploy-
ment, ignoring model monitoring and lacking CT for models.
In other words, these MLOps pipelines can automatically
deploy new models, but model training rounds need to be
manually triggered, e.g., by pull requests to a repository. Kube-

In other words, these MLOps pipelines can automatically
deploy new models, but model training rounds need to be
manually triggered, e.g., by pull requests to a repository. Kube-
ﬂow Pipelines, TensorFlow Extended, Kedro, Metaﬂow, and
ZenML are such open-source frameworks that can orchestrate
MLOps pipelines. However, Kubeﬂow Pipelines, Kedro, and
Metaﬂow focus on model training and deployment, resulting
in MLOps pipelines that ﬂow in a single direction from data
to model training and ﬁnally to model deployment. Although
TensorFlow Extended and ZenML integrate model monitoring
but lack ﬂexibility, only supporting periodic model retraining
and retraining based on the arrival of new data. The periodic
retraining may lack responsiveness to rapidly changing data,

The key functionalities of the CTCD-e MLOps pipeline are
presented in Table I. The software selection (the third column)

The functionalities are based on the previous work outlined
above, emphasizing CT and CD that autonomously adapt in
an experimentation-driven manner. We assume data quality is
covered as a part of a DataOps [13] pipeline.
Model training, deployment, and serving are elementary
activities of an MLOps pipeline and prerequisites for model
CT and CD, hence Func1 and Func4. These functionalities
cover initial activities for shipping the ﬁrst model to production

activities of an MLOps pipeline and prerequisites for model
CT and CD, hence Func1 and Func4. These functionalities
cover initial activities for shipping the ﬁrst model to production
but also activities needed for adapting to changing data, such
as retraining and redeployment. Regarding Func1, training a
model usually requires sequential steps, such as data cleaning,
feature engineering, and the actual training to produce model
artifacts partly covered by DataOps. Similarly, model deploy-
ment may consist of multiple steps, such as model validation.
This multi-step nature necessitates task orchestration, as is
depicted in Func1. The details of these steps are use-case-
speciﬁc, so we do not specify them in detail within our scope.
Tracing model training is essential in MLOps to elevate it
to systematic and traceable engineering practice. ML model
performance can be affected by many factors, such as training
data and tuning parameters. For this reason, Func2 is required

to systematic and traceable engineering practice. ML model
performance can be affected by many factors, such as training

to improve traceability and reproducibility [5]. To provide
better model governance, a central artifact registry is needed
to store generated model artifacts (Func3). Func2 and Func3
also enable version tracking unless covered otherwise, e.g., by
DataOps.
Following the CD4ML framework, we monitor model per-
formance as the retraining trigger (Func5). Model retraining
must be automatically retriggered when model performance
drops (Func6).
A retrained and redeployed model (cf. Func1 and Func4)
must be assessed against its predecessor to determine whether
the new model outperforms its predecessor in production
(Func7). A/B testing can reduce the risk of selecting a worse
model. Finally, as CT and CD consist of multiple phases,
from model training to model comparison in production, a

(Func7). A/B testing can reduce the risk of selecting a worse

from model training to model comparison in production, a

IV . IMPLEMENTA TION
In this section, we ﬁrst outline our choices of the open-
source tools used to implement the CTCD-e MLOps pipeline.
We then present an architecture walkthrough of the pipeline
implementation. The retraining trigger (Func6) and A/B testing
(Func7) as key novel components of the pipeline are elaborated
on after the architecture walkthrough.
A. Decisions on Software Selection
To mitigate the potential vendor lock-in associated with pro-
prietary solutions, our MLOps pipeline needs to be portable,
i.e., installable on various cloud platforms and on-premises en-
vironments. Therefore, we chose Kubernetes as the underlying
platform for hosting our MLOps pipeline.

i.e., installable on various cloud platforms and on-premises en-
vironments. Therefore, we chose Kubernetes as the underlying
platform for hosting our MLOps pipeline.
The pipeline’s tool selection for accomplishing the required
functions is summarized in Table I. To avoid reinventing
the wheel, we used third-party open-source software when-
ever possible. For pipeline components where no suitable
open-source software was directly available, we implemented
them ourselves. Among the third-party software, Kubeﬂow
Pipelines3 offers an SDK that allows developers to create indi-
vidual ML tasks, such as data preprocessing and model ﬁtting,
known as KFP components. These components can then be
organized into an ML workﬂow, referred to as a KFP pipeline.
MLﬂow4 can be deployed as a service and exposes endpoints
for saving model training metadata and model artifacts using
a relational database and standard object storage, for which
we selected PostgreSQL5 and MinIO6, respectively.KServe7

C. Retraining Trigger
Model retraining rounds are triggered by the alerts sent
from Prometheus. Retraining-Triggering Webhook is a speciﬁc
webhook service written in Flask for receiving Prometheus
alerts and calling Kubeﬂow Pipelines to start model retraining.
Prometheus is conﬁgured to evaluate model performance met-
rics scraped from Monitor and generate alerts of deteriorated
metrics, which are sent to Retraining-Triggering Webhook via
HTTP requests. Upon receiving an alert, Retraining-Triggering
Webhook starts a model retraining round by triggering a run-
ning of the corresponding KFP pipeline in Kubeﬂow Pipelines.

D. Production A/B Testing
Model Comparison Runner is another dedicated Flask ser-
vice of the CTCD-e MLOps pipeline responsible for automat-
ically orchestrating A/B tests. For each A/B test, it collects
the required information, generates a speciﬁcation using the
information, and employs the speciﬁcation to invoke Iter8 to
A/B test the performance of a retrained model against the old
model. Three concepts used by Iter8 need to be explained to
understand the functions of Model Comparison Runner.
• Experiment: Experiment is a custom resource deﬁnition

model. Three concepts used by Iter8 need to be explained to
understand the functions of Model Comparison Runner.
• Experiment: Experiment is a custom resource deﬁnition
provided by Iter8. It refers to a way of comparing the
performance of two or more versions of a service. An
experiment can consist of multiple iterations.
• Reward metric: The measured metrics from both services
that determine the winning service.
• Service-level objective (SLO): An SLO describes a per-
formance level a service must achieve. A service loses
A/B testing if it fails to meet an SLO, regardless of its
reward metric.
To launch an experiment, Iter8 users need to create an experi-
ment speciﬁcation in a Y AML ﬁle and use kubectl, the Kuber-
netes command-line tool, to apply the experiment speciﬁcation
to Iter8. An experiment speciﬁcation declares the information
needed for running an experiment, such as model versions in-
volved in the experiment, SLOs, reward metrics, the number of

to Iter8. An experiment speciﬁcation declares the information
needed for running an experiment, such as model versions in-
volved in the experiment, SLOs, reward metrics, the number of
experiment iterations, and the duration of each iteration. Model
Comparison Runner integrates Iter8 into the CTCD-e MLOps
pipeline by automatically creating experiment speciﬁcations
and launching experiments on behalf of pipeline users. For
each served model, an experiment conﬁguration needs to be

Runner in advance. An experiment conﬁguration includes

The alert thresholds were determined based on pilot exper-
iments with the models, where the prediction errors exceeded
the thresholds upon changing the input data. Two different
waiting durations were chosen to cover two scenarios: the short
duration aims to promptly detect model performance degrada-
tion, while the long duration ensures sustained performance
decline, thereby minimizing the risk of false positives due to
temporary ﬂuctuations. For A/B testing in both experiments,
the 95th percentile response latency of the inference services
was used as the SLO, requiring the latency not to exceed 5000
milliseconds. Regarding the reward metric, the smaller MAE,
the better the model performance. Each A/B test consisted of
three iterations, each lasting two minutes.

VI. D ISCUSSION
A. Discussion on the CTCD-e MLOps Pipeline
The presented end-to-end, iterative CTCD-e MLOps
pipeline can autonomously adapt ML models to dynamic
production environments by enabling model CT and CD. The
pipeline is implemented entirely with open-source tools, most
of which are existing third-party tools being more transparent
than proprietary solutions. Due to Kubernetes, the MLOps
pipeline can be deployed in on-premises and cloud environ-
ments, thereby mitigating vendor lock-in challenges when us-
ing proprietary solutions. Our CTCD-e MLOps pipeline is also
generalizable to various ML frameworks: Kubeﬂow Pipelines
is agnostic on ML frameworks, allowing pipeline users to
choose any ML framework to implement their retraining.
KServe is also capable of serving models trained using various
ML frameworks.

is agnostic on ML frameworks, allowing pipeline users to
choose any ML framework to implement their retraining.
KServe is also capable of serving models trained using various
ML frameworks.
Our pipeline also presents two notable novel features for
facilitating ML model CT and CD and, thus, autonomously
improving the adaptivity of an ML system. We developed
and released open-source components to enable these features.
First, Retraining-Triggering Webhook converts an alert gen-
erated by Prometheus into a call for Kubeﬂow Pipelines to
start a model retraining round. This way, pipeline users can
ﬂexibly deﬁne the criteria for triggering model retraining using
Prometheus’ query language PromQL. Below is the PromQL
query used in the wine experiment:

of Kubeﬂow Pipelines and the monitoring stack of Prometheus and Grafana.

Using PromQL, users can aggregate multiple model perfor-
mance metrics and set speciﬁc thresholds for sending alerts.
Additionally, users can specify the pending time for an alert,
which may help avoid unnecessary model retraining if the
performance degradation is only in a tolerably short period.
Second, the Model Comparison Runner integrates Iter8 into
the CD process, which enables pipeline users to A/B test the
performance of a retrained model against its predecessor in
production. The integration of Iter8 enables pipeline users to
progressively roll out a better model and ensure that a retrained
model can be fully deployed only when it outperforms its
predecessor.

production. The integration of Iter8 enables pipeline users to
progressively roll out a better model and ensure that a retrained
model can be fully deployed only when it outperforms its
predecessor.
The pipeline can be extended to other deployment scenarios.
Retraining-Triggering Webhook can be modiﬁed to provide an
endpoint allowing users to manually start a new model training
run in Kubeﬂow Pipelines. This way, users can manually
deploy a new model version and A/B test it against the old
version without needing to wait for the old one to deteriorate.
Besides A/B testing, other common deployment strategies can
be added. In a canary release strategy, the retrained model
will be gradually rolled out as long as it satisﬁes the spec-
iﬁed SLOs. Shadow deployment strategy involves directing
all client trafﬁc to the existing model while simultaneously
copying it to the retrained model, which is particularly useful
when the retrained model requires production-level validation

all client trafﬁc to the existing model while simultaneously
copying it to the retrained model, which is particularly useful
when the retrained model requires production-level validation
before responding to client requests in production. These two
strategies can be added to Model Comparison Runner.
The pipeline users need to conﬁgure and adapt the pipeline
to their ML systems. Foremost, the users are required to
deﬁne their own KFP pipelines for retraining their ML models.
The selected Evidently library only supports tabular data. For
other data types, Monitor can be re-designed to integrate
user-deﬁned monitoring methods. We implemented Joiner for
passing model inputs, outputs, and ground truth to Monitor for
further calculation of model performance metrics. Still, ground
50

truth must be collected from various resources. Collecting
labels and sending them to Joiner are therefore left to pipeline
users. An interesting option is to utilize an existing open-
source feature store tool offering so advanced functionality
that even replaces Joiner. Finally, users can conﬁgure the
parameters related to model monitoring and performance A/B
testing, such as the metrics to be monitored, the retraining
trigger threshold, the SLOs and the number of iterations of
each A/B test, and the duration of each iteration. In conclusion,
our MLOps pipeline does not offer out-of-the-box automation
for model CT or CD. However, compared to non-automated
pipelines and systematic MLOps, the added effort remains
moderate at most.
B. Challenges and Future Directions
Incorporating CT and CD based on model performance into
our MLOps pipeline brought forward the following challenges
that may inspire future research directions. Unlike the litera-

Incorporating CT and CD based on model performance into
our MLOps pipeline brought forward the following challenges
that may inspire future research directions. Unlike the litera-
ture review [3] mentioned in Section II, which focuses on
speciﬁc phases, the challenge we have encountered is related
to the continuous approach as a whole.
Handling delayed or uncertain ground truth and man-
ual labeling.Ground truth is required for monitoring model
functional performance and preparing training data. A chal-
lenge is that ground truth may be delayed or uncertain, and
labeling requires manual work in some use cases. Therefore,
approaches for handling ground truth that is delayed or with
different levels of uncertainty in model monitoring and model
training need to be explored.
Diagnosing model performance deterioration and adap-
tive retraining.Besides detecting model performance issues,
it is necessary to identify their underlying causes, which

Diagnosing model performance deterioration and adap-
tive retraining.Besides detecting model performance issues,
it is necessary to identify their underlying causes, which
may affect how a model is retrained. For example, model
performance can degrade because of outliers. In this case, the
model can be retrained by including the outliers in a historical
training dataset. Model performance can also degrade because
the correlation between the inputs and the ground truth has
changed, though the distribution of the inputs remains the
same. In this case, the model can be simply retrained on
the latest data. More research can be undertaken to explore
how to adapt the model retraining process, e.g., selecting
suitable training datasets based on the root causes of model
performance issues.
Analyzing the performance of retrained models. In
reality, not all model retraining rounds yield improved models.
A retrained model may fail the A/B testing due to poor

performance issues.
Analyzing the performance of retrained models. In
reality, not all model retraining rounds yield improved models.
A retrained model may fail the A/B testing due to poor
selection of retraining data, and issues can also arise from
the inference service rather than the model itself. In such
cases, the old model remains active, and human experts are
required to investigate the reasons when using the CTCD-
e MLOps pipeline. Automating the investigation process of
A/B testing failures is a potential area for further research,
enabling the pipeline to intelligently decide if (and when) it
can autonomously trigger the next retraining iteration or if
human experts need to be involved. More research could also
be conducted to adapt the next retraining round based on the
identiﬁed failure reasons.
Automatic thresholding.When using alerts as a retrain-
ing trigger, setting an appropriate threshold for triggering
an alert is imperative. A too-sensitive threshold may trigger

Automatic thresholding.When using alerts as a retrain-
ing trigger, setting an appropriate threshold for triggering
an alert is imperative. A too-sensitive threshold may trigger
unnecessary retraining and waste computational resources,
while a too-loose threshold may be unresponsive to model
performance issues. Finding an appropriate threshold in a trial-
and-error approach can be time-consuming and burdensome.
Therefore, further research is needed to develop approaches for
suggesting suitable thresholds based on business requirements
and resource constraints.
C. Related Approaches
Online learning [15] is an alternative approach to CT for
adapting ML models to dynamic production environments.
Unlike CT, where models are ﬁt with batch data ofﬂine and
deployed online, online learning aims to update models imme-
diately upon the arrival of each data point in a data stream. Our
CTCD-e MLOps pipeline is not intended for online learning

deployed online, online learning aims to update models imme-
diately upon the arrival of each data point in a data stream. Our
CTCD-e MLOps pipeline is not intended for online learning
use cases. Instead, it is a complementary solution when online
learning is insufﬁcient to guarantee model performance, for
example, due to the noise and outliers of incoming data.
Due to resource constraints, sometimes it may be impossible
to use all historical data to retrain models, leading to the issue
of catastrophic forgetting [16] when retraining deep neural
network models. Catastrophic forgetting refers to the problem
where deep neural network models forget previously learned
information when being trained using the latest data. Continual
learning (CL) [17] is a set of algorithms aiming to address
catastrophic forgetting, and recent research has explored how
to leverage CL in CT for models. Recent studies [18], [19]
provide CT and CD for models, where data drift is used as

catastrophic forgetting, and recent research has explored how
to leverage CL in CT for models. Recent studies [18], [19]
provide CT and CD for models, where data drift is used as
the retraining trigger, and models are then trained using CL
techniques. These two studies focus on neural network models
and provide a CL library for prototyping and evaluating CL
algorithms. Our CTCD-e MLOps pipeline is not limited to
neural network models and allows users to implement various
retraining algorithms, including CL, using Kubeﬂow Pipelines.
Active learning [20] is a set of techniques that select the
most representative data samples from a massive dataset for
manual labeling, thereby reducing the human effort of data
labeling without decreasing the quality of model training.
Incorporating active learning into the training data preparation
may extend model CT to use cases requiring human annota-
tors.
Besides model retraining, several approaches are available

Incorporating active learning into the training data preparation
may extend model CT to use cases requiring human annota-
tors.
Besides model retraining, several approaches are available
to address model performance degradation caused by data
drift. For example, in the case of recurrent data drift, a pre-
trained model trained on the data with a similar distribution
of the drifted data can be retrieved from a model registry
to replace the degraded one, thereby mitigating the effects
of data drift without training a new model each time the
drift occurs. Adaptive ensembles can combine the predictions
of multiple models and dynamically adjust the ensemble
composition based on the observed data drift pattern, thereby

pipeline, it would be valuable to incorporate these alternative
approaches and develop automated techniques for selecting the
most suitable approach to maintain prediction quality when
data drift occurs.
D. Threats to V alidity
Internal validity refers to whether our proposed MLOps
pipeline can provide ﬂexible CT and CD support for mod-
els. We carried out a proof-of-concept evaluation to validate
the pipeline’s capabilities. However, a limitation is that the
pipeline has yet to be used in real-world use cases. The internal
validity could be improved by conducting an empirical case
study involving ML practitioners and real-world data usage
with the pipeline.
External validityconcerns whether our MLOps pipeline can
be generalized to various use cases. To improve the external
validity, we primarily utilized commonly used tools, as re-

with the pipeline.
External validityconcerns whether our MLOps pipeline can
be generalized to various use cases. To improve the external
validity, we primarily utilized commonly used tools, as re-
ﬂected in the number of stars received by the corresponding
GitHub repositories. An exception is Iter8, which is less
mature than other A/B testing tools, such as Flagger13 and
Argo Rollouts14. Despite its relative immaturity, we opted for
Iter8 because of its unique feature of dynamic trafﬁc splitting
using novel algorithms. A threat to the external validity of
our MLOps pipeline is its current limitation to use cases
where ground truth is readily available and the data is tabular.
To address this limitation, we intend to incorporate model
monitoring approaches that can handle delayed ground truth
in the future, as well as enable users to plug their monitoring
algorithms into the pipeline.

We presented an open-source MLOps pipeline that can

in the future, as well as enable users to plug their monitoring

VII. C ONCLUSIONS
We presented an open-source MLOps pipeline that can
autonomously adapt ML systems by continuous training and
deployment (CT and CD). ML models are retrained based
on monitored triggers, and the pipeline leverages automated
A/B testing to perform continuous experimentation as a part
of the deployment to select the better model for serving.
We built the pipeline primarily using existing open-source
components, with any non-existing components being rela-
tively straightforward and released as open-source. Users can
adopt and customize the pipeline to their use cases and ML
systems without speciﬁc knowledge beyond MLOps pipelines
and monitoring. The continuous experimentation loop brings
forward new challenges for highly automated MLOps as future
research directions.
In future work, it would be interesting to expand the evalu-

forward new challenges for highly automated MLOps as future
research directions.
In future work, it would be interesting to expand the evalu-
ation of the pipeline to a wider range of models, particularly
large models, which would provide a more comprehensive
understanding of the time and resource utilization of model
retraining and A/B testing. To further identify the potential
areas of improvement, we consider it beneﬁcial to conduct

maintain model performance against data drift could also be
further explored to improve the pipeline’s efﬁciency.
