3.2. Continual Learning featured in
CLaaS
The increasing demand for overcome classical machine
learning process is leveraging the emergence of new so-
lutions. For a company, CLaaS is a set of toolkit tools
aimed to support the daily work of data scientists and
data engineers in the machine learning development pro-

lutions. For a company, CLaaS is a set of toolkit tools
aimed to support the daily work of data scientists and
data engineers in the machine learning development pro-
cess. In particular, CLaaS provide features thought to
manage the designing, building and managing of repro-
ducible, testable ML-powered software. It is specialized
to guarantee Continuous Monitoring and Training using
Continual Learning solutions. In this subsection, it is de-
scribed how CL methodologies are featured in the CLaaS
paradigm with a brief review.
Detection of data drift distribution . In the classi-
cal ML, the data used in training follows a distribution
supposed stationary. Based on this assumption, the gen-
eralization of model can be limited by the distribution
of data on which it was trained. Nevertheless, In a real-
world application, we have a constant flow of informa-
tion, where the distribution can change due to various
external or internal factors. These changes in the input

world application, we have a constant flow of informa-
tion, where the distribution can change due to various
external or internal factors. These changes in the input
data distribution cause issues in previously trained mod-
els, mainly because their weights are not prepared to
face the drift concerning the training data. This problem
creates the need to detect distribution shifts and update
the model continually.
There are many types of concept drift that have been
identified [21], each of which can affect model perfor-
mance. Different approaches were developed for the
different kinds of shifts. The CLaaS toolkit exploits both
base and advanced approaches. In fact, the user can
select among two main categories, supervised and unsu-
pervised or build custom data drift approaches (for more
detail of data drift solutions see Appendix in [11]).
Continual Learning zoo algorithms . There are
many continual learning strategies in the literature devel-

detail of data drift solutions see Appendix in [11]).
Continual Learning zoo algorithms . There are
many continual learning strategies in the literature devel-
oped for the neural network models. For a more in-depth
overview, we refer the reader to the recent overviews
in [10] and [22]. The latter additionally exposes the bio-
inspired aspects of existing continual approaches. To
roadmap in the CL strategies, it is useful to classify them
into three main groups. First, the Memory-based Contin-
ual Learning Methods, in which gather all methods that
save raw samples as the memory of past tasks in episodic
memory. Second, Architecture-based Continual Learning
Methods, in which they use the model to overcome catas-
trophic forgetting and learn over time. Typically, these
classes of strategies exploit the dynamic neural network
architecture changes that can be explicit or implicit. Fi-
nally, Regularization-based Continual Learning Methods

classes of strategies exploit the dynamic neural network
architecture changes that can be explicit or implicit. Fi-
nally, Regularization-based Continual Learning Methods
consist in modifying the update of weights when learning
in order to keep the memory of previous knowledge. The
literature on CL strategies propose also hybrid solutions
[10].
All these CL strategies allow the update of the model
over time avoiding catastrophic forgetting and are in
CLaaS. In fact, the user of the CLasS can select among
these different continuous learning approaches for dif-
ferent practical scenarios. Most of real-world use cases,
Memory-based Methods can be performant at the cost
of the major memory usage. If data preservation is a
constraint, the user could select CL strategies that are
Memory-free like Architectural or Regularization strate-
gies. Furthermore, in the edge computing context, the
user can select efficient on-device CL strategies like [18]
and [23].

Memory-free like Architectural or Regularization strate-
gies. Furthermore, in the edge computing context, the
user can select efficient on-device CL strategies like [18]
and [23].
Continual Learning metrics . A core feature not
present in machine learning tools for the MLOps pro-
cess is the use of the Continual Learning metric to detect
model of the performance decay over time. It is crucial
to have a set of good evaluation metrics to monitor the
model performance, i.e. the model forgets the past or
does not learn new skills. Detailed on CL evaluation
protocols are well described in [10] [24] [25].
Using Continual Learning strategies in CLaaS, it is
possible to train models in a sequence of tasks and acquire

new knowledge without forgetting what has been trained
in the past. The evaluation criteria have to cover the

It is not enough to observe good final accuracy on an

We should also evaluate how fast it learns and forgets,

4. Continual Brain: System
Architecture and
Implementation
4.1. Design Principles
Continual Brain starts with a simple idea: extend the
library Avalanche as a service model with a set of design
guidelines: modular and independent Building-Block view,
backend flexible and expandable, portability, ease-of-use,
efficiency and scalability. We believe that these principles

guidelines: modular and independent Building-Block view,
backend flexible and expandable, portability, ease-of-use,
efficiency and scalability. We believe that these principles
are important first steps for reliable Continual Learning
tools in real-world applications.
Modular and independent Building-Block view
The main design principle for Continual Brain follows
from the concept of modular building blocks, the idea of
providing a set of services independent of each other and
a baseline for further improvements. This particular fo-
cus on module independence is maintained to guarantee
the stand-alone individual module services. Moreover,
the user can pick up a particular set of services and make
use of a customize the others. For instance, the user can
select CL strategy services and customize services of CL
metrics or CL models.
Backend flexible and expandable Continual Brain
is based on the principle of the API interface indepen-

select CL strategy services and customize services of CL
metrics or CL models.
Backend flexible and expandable Continual Brain
is based on the principle of the API interface indepen-
dent of the backend and easy to extend. Therefore, it
is possible for the maintainers to expand the backend
and add more services API exposed to the user. In this
way, it allows also to employ of different environments,
technologies and methods and guarantees flexibility of
execution.
Portability A critical design objective of Continual
Brain is to allow experimental results to be seamlessly
portable in both edge and cloud resources. As the first
step, we have decided to allow data scientist and engineer
to simply integrate their own research and code into
the platform to speed up the development of original
continual learning solutions in practical scenarios.
Efficiency and scalabilityThese two designing prin-
ciples are fundamental in modern DL research experi-

continual learning solutions in practical scenarios.
Efficiency and scalabilityThese two designing prin-
ciples are fundamental in modern DL research experi-
ments. Like current DL frameworks, we offer the end-
user a seamless and transparent experience regardless of

Ease-of-use The last principle presented is the focus

4.2. System Workflow
The complete workflow of Continual Brain system can
be split into two phases, the offline preparation phase
and the online execution and monitoring phase.
In the offline phase, researchers first leverage the built-
in APIs or SDK of the CL zoo strategies to use or cus-
tomize a CL model. Meanwhile, engineers can prepare
a configuration file to set up the trigger rule and the

in APIs or SDK of the CL zoo strategies to use or cus-
tomize a CL model. Meanwhile, engineers can prepare
a configuration file to set up the trigger rule and the
frequency of the retrain/fine-tuning according to our
provided template.
In the online monitoring phase, the system follows
the predefined rules in the configuration file to schedule
model updating tasks. The main operation of monitor-
ing model performance is based on CL metrics and it is
executed in an automated manner with the user’s config-
uration rules.
4.3. Architecture
The architecture of Continual Brain can be split into three
main layers: Interface, Middleware, CL-Backend. Further
internal components and frameworks model the overall
structure, while the middle-layer endpoints are linked to
the outermost components.
Interface, written in Flask python micro-framework,
and helps the data scientist to interact with the CL-
Backend. In particular, this layer manages communica-

the outermost components.
Interface, written in Flask python micro-framework,
and helps the data scientist to interact with the CL-
Backend. In particular, this layer manages communica-
tion with clients in RESTful APIs way or through an SDK
(see Figure 4). This top layer interfaces with custom user
code to run experiments or evaluate the performance
with data tests.
Middleware, a Python package that manages the
monitoring and update stages, and controls model ver-
sioning. Moreover, this middle layer is set up as a proxy
to access external storage services or for lower-level stor-
age. In this way, the logic of the lower-level storage is
decoupled and flexible from the user storage preferences,
making it possible also multiple storage services for the
same user. These middle-level components allow also ser-
vices external to the application. The latter can be either
integrated into the application or completely external.
For example, data storage services and the database can

vices external to the application. The latter can be either
integrated into the application or completely external.
For example, data storage services and the database can
be provided in the CL-backend or can be out cloud ser-
vices. Here, it is not used libraries but a custom codebase
implementation.
CL-Backend, leverages the Avalanche CL library [13]
and PyTorch machine learning framework. The func-
tionality of the Avalanche library in the Continual Brain

5.1. Experiment Setup
All experiments are conducted with the following set-
tings. We use common CL benchmark named CORe50
[28] for Computer Vision object recognition application.
We also employ an instance of fashion analysis named
categories prediction to evaluate both performance and

As Figures 7 and 8 show, the strategies based on Con-
tinual Learning are very efficient at the cost of a small
decay in predictive performance. As predicted, the Cu-
mulative strategies achieve similar results of the upper

and memory saved by the CL approaches are up to a

These results demonstrate that the automation of
the ML pipeline can be achieved efficiently and in an
adaptable manner with this CL-tool. Therefore, in non-
stationary production environments CL strategies might
prove essential to attain efficient stateful training.
6. Conclusion and Discussion
In this paper, we have described CLaaS, a novel software
as a service and licensing model mainly based on a contin-
ual learning approach. A first version, named Continual
Brain, has been implemented by extending the function-
alities of Avalanche as a set of micro-services. We have
demonstrated the usability and efficiency of the system
with representative case studies in machine vision and
fashion domains.
Nowadays,the industrial and company applications
could help to direct new CL research directions toward
more practical scenarios. There are various practical
fields that researchers and companies have investigated
with Continual Learning. The literature has proposed

more practical scenarios. There are various practical
fields that researchers and companies have investigated
with Continual Learning. The literature has proposed
different CL real-world applications. For instance, in
MLOps, surveillance videos, robotics and machine vision,
clinical and medical sector, industry and edge computing.
Anyway, there are also many real-world and application-
research directions unexplored with Continual Learning.
Prevalent scenarios of Continual Learning are Class
Incremental Learning (CIL). They assume disjoint sets
of classes as tasks but are less realistic in a real-world
application. Recent works [30] and [31] focus the atten-
tion to address a more realistic CL setup which occurs
frequently in real world AI deployment scenarios. The
request to practical CL setting seems also start from the
companies that use CL for core business.
Finally, deploying CL to real-world applications is still
quite challenging. The absence of CL tools in ML sys-

companies that use CL for core business.
Finally, deploying CL to real-world applications is still
quite challenging. The absence of CL tools in ML sys-
tem workflow and the poor investigation of real-world
applications are the main reasons. We believe the CLaaS
paradigm can narrow the gap between research and its in-
dustrial application, and speed up companies innovative
trend towards Continual Learning.

ABSTRACT
With the growth and expansion of the internet, web attacks have become more powerful and pose
a significant threat in the cyber world. In response to this, this paper presents a deceptive approach

With the growth and expansion of the internet, web attacks have become more powerful and pose
a significant threat in the cyber world. In response to this, this paper presents a deceptive approach
for gathering malicious behavior to understand the strategies used by web attackers. The harmful
requests collected through cyber traps or honeypots are analyzed and used to train machine learn-
ing (ML) models for web attack detection. Additionally, we implement an ML operations (MLOps)
pipeline to automate the continuous training and deployment of these ML models in defensive sys-
tems. This pipeline trains the production model with newly collected data by using predefined trig-
gers. Our experiments on two datasets, including Fwaf and our own, demonstrate that a proactive
and continuous approach to tracking adversary behavior can effectively detect zero-day attacks,
such as CVE-2022-26134 in web application servers.

Key words: Web attack detection, Cyber trap, Cyber attack detection, Cyber deception
INTRODUCTION
Daily life has seen a significant increase in the volume
and speed of data since the advancement of informa-
tion technology. Furthermore, the Internet has trans-
formed traditional methods of daily life. W eb applica-
tions have become the most widely used applications
on the Internet. W eb applications are used in differ-
ent areas and are important in people’ s daily routines,
especially as more individuals transfer their applica-
tions and personal information to the cloud. Due to
the widespread use of web applications and the signif-
icant amount of personal data saved on servers, they
become attractive targets for attacks.
A recent report about cybersecurity 1 incidents illus-
trated that 75% of attacks are detected in the applica-

become attractive targets for attacks.
A recent report about cybersecurity 1 incidents illus-
trated that 75% of attacks are detected in the applica-
tion layer, while web servers are the main targets of
hackers. There are two reasons why adversaries are
inclined to intrude and break into web servers. First,
because large amounts of private data are stored in
server databases, attackers can profit significantly by
selling that data. Second, the ability to inject mali-
cious code into server source files allows attackers to
hack and manipulate users who browse or download
these documents.
Obviously, safeguarding web applications from intru-
sions is essential. Typically, attacks are detected pri-
marily based on recognizable characteristics. This
method is useful for detecting known attack types,
but it necessitates human involvement in collecting
and analyzing attack data samples for identification.
Consequently, identity-based website attack detection

but it necessitates human involvement in collecting
and analyzing attack data samples for identification.
Consequently, identity-based website attack detection
(W AD) is no longer adequate for detecting attacks
with novel exploits.
Additionally, with the rapid and significant advance-
ment in the field of ML, these algorithms have demon-
strated their effectiveness in numerous fields, includ-
ing web attack detection. Some research has achieved
desired results when incorporating ML into web pro-
tection systems. T o automatically detect denial-of-
service (DoS) attacks, Francisco et al. 2 proposed an
ML model to make inferences based on signatures
previously extracted from samples of network traffic.
The results of this model on four modern benchmark
datasets have achieved an online detection rate (DR)
of attacks above 96%.
In the same domain of W AD, Lianget al. 3 proposed a
deep learning-based approach to classify anomalous
requests. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) were

of attacks above 96%.
In the same domain of W AD, Lianget al. 3 proposed a
deep learning-based approach to classify anomalous
requests. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) were
used to learn patterns of normal requests using only
unsupervised normal requests. Then, a neural net-
work classifier that takes the outputs from the RNNs
as input was trained in a supervised manner to dif-
ferentiate between anomalous and normal requests.
Similarly, Y unyiet al. 4 suggested a W AD that applied
a long short-term memory (LSTM) network to ana-
lyze the malicious intentions hidden in user actions.
The experimental results on the CSIC 2010 dataset
achieved an accuracy of 99.87%.
In addition, the ML approach for detecting many
attack types needs administrators or data scientists

The main contributions in this article are summarized

DESIGN
This section describes our approach to create an ML-
based W AD with cyber traps to be proactive in collect-
ing training datasets for the ML process. After that, we
integrate the MLOps pipeline in our system detection
to enhance automation in the deployment and main-
tenance of ML models.

Kubeflow, Data Storage, Honeypot, and Serving W eb-
site, as shown in Figure 1. Each of these parts per-
forms a different task and executes on separate hosts.

B. ML-based Web Attack Detection
In this study, our W AD model is built based on the en-

eralization performance of using a single algorithm14.
In the ensemble model, we use 3 well-known ML algo-

eralization performance of using a single algorithm14.
In the ensemble model, we use 3 well-known ML algo-
rithms in classification, including support vector ma-
chine (SVM), logistic regression (LR), and K-nearest
neighbors (KNN). In addition, we also built a convo-
lutional neural network (CNN) model to compare the

neural network.
Ensemble Learning (Stacking Classifier)
model
Stacking, also known as a voting classifier, is an en-
semble learning method. This method improves the
model’ s performance by combining different ML clas-
sifiers for classification. Majority voting, a popular
voting technique, is used in this approach. It has three
scenarios based on unanimous voting, simple major-
ity voting, and plurality voting. Furthermore, hard
voting usually refers to plurality voting.

C. MLOps for Web Attack Detection
In this study, we proposed a methodology for a W AD-
based MLOps pipeline to create an automatic train-
ing rule for continuously updating ML models. The
pipeline workflow is shown in Figure 3.
Figure 3 : The workflow of WAD in integration
with the MLOps pipeline.
The Log Collection Process
The log collection process as in Figure 4 is explained
as follows:

Figure 3 : The workflow of WAD in integration
with the MLOps pipeline.
The Log Collection Process
The log collection process as in Figure 4 is explained
as follows:
The honeypot’ s daily process is located outside the in-
ternet to collect logs.
After an activation signal from Kubeflow, Honeypot
turns on the firewall allowing only administrator ac-
cess, and each step of the log processing is executed
sequentially:
• Collect good requests: the process of simulating
web page interactions as normal users to collect
labeled datasets is normal.
• Collect bad requests: This process will collect
the malicious exploit code attacked by the ad-
ministrator.
• Parse and push log: process collected logs and
send them to the Data storage.
Figure 4: Log Collection Process.
Data Acquisition and Processing
Data acquisition is responsible for collecting enough
data to train the ML model. The tasks for data acqui-
sition and processing can be summarized as follows:

Data Acquisition and Processing
Data acquisition is responsible for collecting enough
data to train the ML model. The tasks for data acqui-
sition and processing can be summarized as follows:
• Data Extraction and Analysis : Analyzing data
to understand the data schema and integrating
relevant data to maximize model performance.
• Data Preparation : is responsible for cleaning
and splitting datasets into training, validation,
and test sets. For instance, these outliers de-
tected by the local outlier factor algorithm will
be removed from the dataset, or NULL values
are transformed to zero. This step results in for-
matted data for training ML models.
When there is not enough data to train or retrain the
ML model, two main approaches allow for overcom-
ing the issue as follows:
• Data Augmentation : This technique aims to
balance non-IID (nonindependent and identi-
cally distributed) data. More specifically, when
the difference ratio between the attack and nor-

• Data Augmentation : This technique aims to
balance non-IID (nonindependent and identi-
cally distributed) data. More specifically, when
the difference ratio between the attack and nor-
mal labels is more than 20%, the conditional
generative adversarial network (CGAN) will
generate data with a minority label to keep the
difference within 20%.

• Incremental Learning (IL): which reuses most
of the weights of a trained neural network.
Training, Testing, and Deploying the ML (ML)
Model
The process of training ML models is an iterative pro-
cess in which data scientists work with several algo-
rithms, data features, and hyperparameters. The out-
put of this step is a set of metrics for evaluating the
quality of the model. Once the best ML model has
been chosen, it is saved and deployed to the serving
website. Our primary objective is to ensure that we
monitor all testing experiments, provide the reusabil-
ity of code, and uphold the ability to continue updat-
ing the robustness of ML-based W AD.
Building Honeypot System and Service
Website
Honeypot System Overview
In this study, we apply the W eb Honeypot to simu-
late specific web services and attract particular types
of attacks by specific web technologies, as in Figure 5.

Website
Honeypot System Overview
In this study, we apply the W eb Honeypot to simu-
late specific web services and attract particular types
of attacks by specific web technologies, as in Figure 5.
Figure 5: Overview of the honeypot system.
Honeypot runs potentially attractive web applications
on the Nginx server using Java programming lan-
guage. The system records all requests to the above
web applications. At the same time, honeypots are al-
ways listening for trigger signals from Kuberflow to
perform tasks of collecting datasets, processing data,
and pushing data to Data Storage.
Trigger Processing
A trigger is a flag that tells the system when a recurring
run configuration spawns a new run. The following
types of run triggers are available:
• Access Management: allows objects based on
the IP address to access the web application.
• Nginx website server management : format-
ting log data by getting the properties from the
HTTP request packet sent by the user, as in Fig-
ure 6.

the IP address to access the web application.
• Nginx website server management : format-
ting log data by getting the properties from the
HTTP request packet sent by the user, as in Fig-
ure 6.
• Access Log Processing : As shown in Fig-
ure 7, each formatted access log is classi-
fied and sorted, and duplicates in the above-
formatted dataset are removed. Finally, both
goodrequest.txt and badrequest.txt files are
pushed to the Data Storage.
Deploying ML Models to the Serving Website:
W e are constructing a serving website designed in Fig-
ure 8, which continuously listens for updating new
machine learning models. Additionally, the serving
website will receive files in both.txt and.csv formats,
containing good and bad requests. Subsequently, the
serving website employs a machine learning model to
classify these requests. T o achieve the goal of detect-
ing new attacks and avoiding false negatives, we se-
lected a detection threshold of 0.5 for identifying bad

classify these requests. T o achieve the goal of detect-
ing new attacks and avoiding false negatives, we se-
lected a detection threshold of 0.5 for identifying bad
requests. Therefore, the predicted label of the detec-
tion model is 1 (indicating a bad request) if the pre-
dicted value is greater than 0.5, and vice versa.

tion of the MLOps pipeline in a variety of scenarios.
First, we describe data resources and partitions. Then,
we concentrate on experimental settings, such as en-
vironmental conditions, baseline studies, and perfor-
mance metrics. Finally, we ran a series of experiments
to compare the MLOps pipeline’ s performance with
various ML models.

Feature extraction
In the W AD scenario, we use queries or requests to de-
tect attacks. Furthermore, we extract all records based
on the TF-IDF (T erm Frequency - Inverse Document
Frequency) numerical statistical method. As in Fig-
ure 9, we count the frequency of each word in every re-
quest (TF) and count the number of queries/requests
in which this word occurs (IDF). These words are the
features of the dataset that are equal to the ratio of TF
and IDF .

Fwaf - Web-Application Firewall dataset
Our experiments are also evaluated on the Fwaf
dataset. In this dataset, all collected records contain
benign traffic and the most up-to-date common at-
tacks related to real-world scenarios. The data were
labeled by two types: Bad and Good. Approximately
1,350,000 records have been captured and processed
for CSV structure format. Furthermore, with the ra-
tio of good and bad labels less than 2.5%, as shown
in Table 2, the Fwaf dataset is completely a non-IID
dataset.

CONCLUSION
T o effectively detect unknown web attacks, ML mod-
els must be trained using a large, up-to-date dataset.
As such, deceptive tactics such as installing honeypots
and cyber traps within the network can prove to be
a valuable strategy for attracting attackers to exploit
vulnerable servers. The logs and requests obtained
from these deceptive resources can then be used to
train the ML-based web attack detection model. The
experimental results on two datasets demonstrate the
feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method

train the ML-based web attack detection model. The
experimental results on two datasets demonstrate the
feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed method
for constructing high-performance machine learning
models for detecting web attacks. In particular, our
ensemble model also proves more efficient and sta-
ble than SVM, KNN, LR and CNN and is perfectly
suitable for deployment on the Serving website. Fur-
thermore, by utilizing triggers defined in the MLOps
pipeline, these models can be regularly updated with
new indicators identified in the cyber trap system, en-
abling them to recognize harmful requests directed
toward web servers. Our experiments involving the
setup of a web honeypot, an MLOps pipeline, and
evaluations on two datasets demonstrate the benefits
of proactive, deceptive, and ongoing approaches in
the realm of web attack detection.

ABSTRACT
∗Machine learning engineering is an important technology that has
attracted the attention of academia and industry in the past two
years. For AI to become a productivity of enterprises, it must be
engineered to solve the problems of model development, deploy-
ment, management, prediction, and other lifecycle management,
and MLOps system is the most concerned technology in the cur-
rent machine learning engineering process. This paper starts from
the training evolution process of intelligent computing models in

and MLOps system is the most concerned technology in the cur-
rent machine learning engineering process. This paper starts from
the training evolution process of intelligent computing models in
machine learning engineering, focuses on the influence of data,
training algorithms, and requirement indicators on the model evo-
lution process, and gives the definition of Model Provenance and its
evolutionary correlation elements. Based on the concept of model
lineage, this paper designs a method to manage the model evolution
process in the MLOps system and implements the related system.

2 BACKGROUND
2.1 MLOps and AI Engineering
Unlike traditional software systems that focus only on business
logic code, a typical intelligent computing application contains
three elements: data, model, and business code. The model, as the
core of the intelligent computing application, needs to be influenced
by both data and business changes. The relationship between the
three is shown in Figure 1. Data is used for model training, and
the model is used to drive business operations after training is
completed. Each change in feedback during this process results in a
change in the performance of the intelligent computing application.
With the development and improvement of machine learning
and deep learning technologies, artificial intelligence has moved
out of the laboratory and into the production environment. How-
ever, in the process of applying machine learning models to actual

and deep learning technologies, artificial intelligence has moved
out of the laboratory and into the production environment. How-
ever, in the process of applying machine learning models to actual
production, there is a model drift problem that cannot be ignored,
which makes the performance of machine learning models devi-
ate from people’s expectations (i.e., business goals). Model drift
usually refers to the deterioration of model predictions over time,
and is divided into two categories: concept drift and data drift. The
goal of MLOps is to introduce the traditional software engineering
concept of DevOps into the field of machine learning, and to sup-
port the continuous integration and continuous release of machine
learning data, models and business through an automated or semi-
automated workflow, so as to provide a better understanding of the
business, models and data. The goal is to build an effective linkage
mechanism between changes in any one element and the other

automated workflow, so as to provide a better understanding of the
business, models and data. The goal is to build an effective linkage
mechanism between changes in any one element and the other
two elements, and to promote the continuous improvement of the
performance and quality of intelligent computing applications.
2.2 Model Provenance
In the field of data science, data provenace has always been a re-
search point that has received attention. The data is described
throughout the process, and the process evolved over time, its ap-
plication is wide, including data quality evaluation, data check, data
recovery, and data reference. The data is generally divided into
data evolution processes between different data sources and the
data evolution process inside the same data source. The foregoing
mentioned that the data played a basic role in the intelligent com-
puting application development process, and the changes in data

data evolution process inside the same data source. The foregoing
mentioned that the data played a basic role in the intelligent com-
puting application development process, and the changes in data
will result in changes in model performance to a certain extent. In
order to meet the changes in business needs, the machine learning
algorithm engineers will adjust the data set or optimization model

3 DESIGN AND IMPLEMENTATION
3.1 Affecting Factors of Model Provenance
In machine learning, models are at the heart of the most attention.
A machine learning model is often described as a file that has been
trained to recognize certain types of patterns. From this definition,
we can see several typical properties of machine learning models.
• Persistability. A model is stored as a file, i.e., it can exist for
a long time and can be parsed according to certain rules for
model reuse.

• Persistability. A model is stored as a file, i.e., it can exist for
a long time and can be parsed according to certain rules for
model reuse.
• Need to be trained. A model is a product of training, and
the ability of a model to recognize certain types of patterns
comes from the training process.
• Ability to be bounded. At least so far, models in machine
learning can only be used to recognize specific patterns and
do not yet have general intelligence.
The persistence of machine learning models is the basis of model
provenance. By deserializing model files, a model can be used for
inference tasks or for further training (e.g., model update, migration
learning, etc.).
The process of model training is the driver of model provenance.
The essence of training is the process of extracting sample data
features and revising the parameters in the model so that the model
can better recognize the patterns present in the data. The model

The essence of training is the process of extracting sample data
features and revising the parameters in the model so that the model
can better recognize the patterns present in the data. The model
training process usually introduces a series of optimization meth-
ods to accelerate the process of minimizing the loss function, and
different training optimization methods may have different effects
on the model performance and training efficiency.
The model capability boundary can be used as a marker to de-
termine the different stages of model provenance. The capability
boundary is usually specified by a specific business goal, and the
change of model capability boundary (including functional bound-
ary, performance boundary, robustness boundary, etc.) is the pro-
cess of the model reaching a specific metric.
It can be considered that the evolution of a machine learning
model is a process in which indicators, data, and training methods

cess of the model reaching a specific metric.
It can be considered that the evolution of a machine learning
model is a process in which indicators, data, and training methods
work together as shown in Figure 2. Indicators set the target bound-
aries for model training evolution, data are the basis for whether
the model training process can achieve the indicators, and train-
ing methods are the determinants of whether the model training
process is efficient.
The model provenance may continue to change with changes
in indicators, data, and training methods, especially when changes
in indicators may cause the model to branch. For example, when
functional indicators change, which may result in existing data as
well as training methods no longer being applicable to the new indi-
cators, it may cause fundamental changes in the model, resulting in
model branching. In contrast, simple changes in dataset expansion
or labeling methods, as well as changes in training methods, may

cators, it may cause fundamental changes in the model, resulting in
model branching. In contrast, simple changes in dataset expansion
or labeling methods, as well as changes in training methods, may
affect the achievement of performance indicators more.
3.2 Design and Implementation
3.2.1 Design Objectives. The core objective of model provenance
management is to manage the evolution of a model developed
and trained in a machine learning task. However, considering that
model evolution is influenced by several relatively independent but
closely related factors, managing and maintaining model versions
alone does not reflect the process of model evolution, and model
provenance should include all factors that influence the process of
model evolution. Therefore, model provenance management should
cover the following objectives:
• Training requirements and indicators management. The first
goal of model family management is to manage the require-

cover the following objectives:
• Training requirements and indicators management. The first
goal of model family management is to manage the require-
ments and objectives of machine learning tasks. On the one
hand, the requirements of machine learning tasks directly
affect the acquisition of data sets and the selection of model
structures (e.g., regression or classification, decision trees
or neural networks, convolutional or recurrent neural net-
works, etc.), and on the other hand, the requirements of
metrics determine the termination conditions of the model
training process. Therefore, the management of model train-
ing requirements and indicators requirements is the starting
point of model provenance management, and runs through
the whole life cycle of model evolution.
• Dataset management. Datasets frame the upper bound of the
final effect that can be achieved for model training, and in
the existing machine learning theory, models cannot break

• Dataset management. Datasets frame the upper bound of the
final effect that can be achieved for model training, and in
the existing machine learning theory, models cannot break
through the feature constraints in datasets. In the last two
years, more and more data scientists and data engineers
have adjusted the process of engineering machine learning
from model-centric to data-centric, and it can be said that
dataset management is the most important factor of model
provenance management.
• Training algorithm management. On the one hand, for the
same dataset and the same model structure, selecting differ-
ent training algorithms may lead to different training results,
such as model convergence speed, precision, and recall rate
may be different. On the other hand, due to the different
requirements and indicators of the target platform for model
application, a series of new optimization methods are also
introduced, such as compression methods in the field of deep

requirements and indicators of the target platform for model
application, a series of new optimization methods are also
introduced, such as compression methods in the field of deep
learning such as quantization methods, all of which can lead
to changes in the model evolution path. Therefore, training
algorithm management is also an important aspect of model
provenance management to be considered.
• Model performance management. Model performance re-
flects the model training effect, especially in the actual pro-
duction environment model performance changes often be-
come an important reason for the adjustment of model train-
ing needs and indicators requirements, and is the main con-
tributor to the formation of continuous model evolution, so it
is necessary to incorporate model performance management
into the scope of model provenance management.

The model provenance maintenance sub-system consists of the
following main components:
• Code tracking module. It collects the model code repository
in machine learning tasks through the interface in the MLOps
system, tracks the version updates of model code and training
algorithm code, and submits the code version information
to the model provenance constructor for model provenance
construction.
• Data set monitor. Through the interface in MLOps system,
we monitor the changes of dataset, including dataset ex-
pansion, dataset widening, data annotation, etc., and record
the dataset version and mark the changes between different
dataset versions in conjunction with the dataset selection
of training jobs. For MLOps systems with dataset version
management capability, the dataset versions are obtained

dataset versions in conjunction with the dataset selection
of training jobs. For MLOps systems with dataset version
management capability, the dataset versions are obtained
directly from the system.
• Job tracking module. It tracks the model training and valida-
tion jobs running in the MLOps system, focusing on obtain-
ing the dataset used for training, code version, and training
process status, and submits the relevant information to the
model family constructor for model family construction.
• Model monitor. In conjunction with the job tracking module,
the model monitor monitors the model release in the MLOps
system. The model release is generally released after the
model has been trained and validated, and the model monitor
keeps an eye on the additions and deletions of the model
and records this information for model family construction.
• Model provenance constructor. By integrating and analyzing
the information collected from the above four modules, the

and records this information for model family construction.
• Model provenance constructor. By integrating and analyzing
the information collected from the above four modules, the
model provenance is continuously constructed and persisted
to the model family database.
• Model provenance database. A graph database for storing
model provenance information. From the analysis in sub-
section 3.1, it is easy to find that the model provenance is
a directed acyclic graph, so it is stored through the graph
database.
• Model performance database. In conjunction with the per-
formance estimator in the runtime maintenance sub-system,
the performance of a particular version of the model is per-
sisted over time. A temporal database is used to implement
the storage of performance data and to associate it with a
specific model version in the model provenance database.

5 CONCLUSIONS
This paper first analyzes the design risks and challenges in the
process of engineering machine learning, focusing on the many un-
controllable aspects of model-centric machine learning tasks from
development to use in production environments. After that, the
idea of implementing model lineage management in the process of
machine learning application development is proposed, and the def-
inition and influencing factors of model lineage are given. Finally, a

machine learning application development is proposed, and the def-
inition and influencing factors of model lineage are given. Finally, a
model lineage management system for MLOps system is designed
and implemented, which obtains the code, dataset, model and train-
ing/deployment status information in MLOps system through the
interface, and constructs model lineage database and model perfor-
mance database, which provides a new technical support for the
engineering application of machine learning. We will present our
detail research in future paper.

Abstract—Continuously learning and serving from evolving
streaming data and serving in real-time is a challenging problem.
Traditionally, data is partitioned and processed in batches to
train machine learning (ML) models. In industrial applications,

streaming data and serving in real-time is a challenging problem.
Traditionally, data is partitioned and processed in batches to
train machine learning (ML) models. In industrial applications,
static models’ performance drops over time (model degradation,
concept drift), requiring new models to be trained with recent
data and redeployed in production. The scientific community
has been studying online and adaptive methods to address
batch-learning limitations and continuously train AI tasks for
industrial applications such as cyber-security, AIOps, anomaly
scoring, and drift detection in stock markets. This paper deals
with the MLOps aspects of deploying such online and dynamic
models to address the requirements in the production systems
for real-time applications. Our architectures - based on open-
source tools such as Kafka and River - demonstrated how online
learning methods could be scaled horizontally in production to
meet the demands of a high-velocity streaming pipeline. We

I. INTRODUCTION
Data streaming learning [1], also known as online learning
[2], refers to continuously learning from new data and making
predictions in an online fashion. MLOps can be defined
as the set of practices (data modelling, data engineering,
data processing, DevOps) and tools that aim to deploy and
maintain machine learning models in production on top of
a scalable and automated environment. We refer to MLOps
as the process of operationalizing and maintaining machine
learning in production [3]. Most of the methods released by
the scientific community or models used in industry neglect

as the process of operationalizing and maintaining machine
learning in production [3]. Most of the methods released by
the scientific community or models used in industry neglect
that streaming data are evolving in real-world scenarios, and
feature distributions are evolving [4]. A majority of models or
MLOps (Machine Learning Operations) platforms [5] typically
make a strong assumption (either explicitly or implicitly) that
the data distribution is stationary, therefore, ignoring the limits
of batch ML in production [6]. This provides motivation to
consider online machine learning models, which are dynam-
ically updated after each processed observation, without the
need to retrain frequently on batched data stored in memory.
In industrial environments, where streaming data is produced
with evolving dynamics, there is a real need for architectures
that manage machine learning (ML) models in production
[7], that adapt to shifting data distributions [4], that deal

with evolving dynamics, there is a real need for architectures
that manage machine learning (ML) models in production
[7], that adapt to shifting data distributions [4], that deal
with complex real-time application scenarios, and are self-
maintaining. This work is motivated by the following question:
Given application events produced in streaming, where
both the number and the type of the features are continu-
ously evolving (ex. heterogeneous logs), how can we train
incrementally & serve dynamic models in production without
pausing the inference pipeline for real-time constraints ?
The problem we address in this work is how to build a
system to operationalize dynamic online models, which learn
and predict continuously for data streams. Developing and de-
ploying machine learning systems is relatively fast and cheap,
but maintaining them over time is difficult and expensive
[8]. The unpredictability of online learning algorithms, due

ploying machine learning systems is relatively fast and cheap,
but maintaining them over time is difficult and expensive
[8]. The unpredictability of online learning algorithms, due
to the dynamic and evolving nature of the model, exacerbates
the technical and functional costs of MLOps under real-time
constraints, aka Continuous MLOps.
Typically, streaming and online models from the scien-

no simple and straightforward way to use such research-

key challenge here is to make the model operationalization
(MLOps) compliant with the constraints imposed by existing

Despite the fact that online learning models carry a sig-
nificant promise in various domains such as big data [9],

banking [13], agriculture [14], telecommunication [15], cloud
computing [16], and healthcare [17], the lack of proper mech-
anisms to deploy such models in production has stalled their
adoption. The two main factors holding back adoption in the

anisms to deploy such models in production has stalled their
adoption. The two main factors holding back adoption in the
industry are the lack of available labels at prediction time and
the risk involved with updating models, which serve critical
production and real-time requests without guarantees. Another
factor is the specific requirements of a complete and robust
streaming architecture that provides data to the models in real-
time and is key for handling predictions and model updates.
Our work aims to address the aforementioned challenges and
provide a practical solution to operationalize online learning
within industrial settings.
Advantages of online learning (single pass, incremental)
compared to batch learning (multiple passes) : The traditional
approach to machine learning (batch) assumes that data is
(1) always available and (2) fully represents the learning
task or problem, i.e. data distribution is static. However,
there are cases where keeping all the data in memory is

(1) always available and (2) fully represents the learning
task or problem, i.e. data distribution is static. However,
there are cases where keeping all the data in memory is
unfeasible and/or impractical. Single-pass and incremental
learning methods are similar in the sense that they only see the
data once. A single-pass method learns from observing only
once single instances, mini-batches or large batches of data;
however, they are not required to provide predictions during
this process. On the other hand, incremental methods mainly
focus on learning from single instances and are explicitly
designed to provide predictions as they learn (update their
internal model of the data as seen so far). Additionally,
recent incremental methods are designed to handle changes
in data distribution, i.e. they adapt to changes in the data
that represent the learning task. Data availability, resource
management, and model availability (capable of prediction

in data distribution, i.e. they adapt to changes in the data
that represent the learning task. Data availability, resource
management, and model availability (capable of prediction
during training) are important challenges that require specific
designs for learning methods. In practice, new batch learning
methods are continuously trained and replaced in streaming
environments. This is fine for multiple real-world problems,
especially if data is static or changes slowly over time (models
are replaced when they start to underperform). However, batch
learning methods are not designed for this scenario and can
not be easily modified to work in an incremental fashion.

II. RELATED WORK : ONLINE LEARNING AND MLO PS
Online machine learning [2] builds stream models, which
are updated based on information mined from the most recent
examples. Unlike classical batch, machine learning models,
trained once and then deployed in production, online learn-
ing algorithms operate in a streaming setting. StreamAI [7]
presents how to deal with the industrial challenges of Contin-
ual Learning Systems in Production and AI Industrialization
at scale. It covers the stream learning setting and the issues
raised by online machine learning models during the complete
ML & MLOps journey (building, deployment, maintenance).
This work aims to address some of the industrial challenges

raised by online machine learning models during the complete
ML & MLOps journey (building, deployment, maintenance).
This work aims to address some of the industrial challenges
presented in [7] for practical online learning and serving.
A. Online Machine Learning for Data Streams
A set of open-source tools for mining big data streams
are available on a variety of platforms. MOA [20], the most
popular stream learning library in Java, contains an extensive
set of methods for multiple tasks, including classification, re-
gression, drift detection, clustering, anomaly detection, active
learning, etc. SAMOA [21] provides distributed implementa-
tions of stream learning algorithms that run on top of stream
processing engines such as Apache Storm, Apache S4, Apache
Samza, and Apache Flink. StreamDM [22] is designed to
work with Spark Streaming. V owpal Wabbit 2 is a machine
learning system which pushes the frontier of machine learning

Samza, and Apache Flink. StreamDM [22] is designed to
work with Spark Streaming. V owpal Wabbit 2 is a machine
learning system which pushes the frontier of machine learning
with techniques such as online, hashing, reductions, learn-
ing2search, active, and interactive learning. There is a specific
focus on reinforcement learning with several contextual bandit
algorithms implemented and the online nature lending to the
problem well. River [18] is an open-source library resulting
from the merge of the two most popular Python packages for
online machine learning: creme [23] and scikit-multiflow [24].
River3 is popular both in academia and industry as it leverages
the popularity of the Python language and the richness of its
ecosystem (classification, clustering regression, multi-output
drift detection and models pipelines). All these tools, coming
from research labs, are intended for data stream processing.

Online machine learning methods have been extensively
studied in recent literature, including applications for data

transformation techniques [26], classification [25], clustering

III. PROBLEM STATEMENT : ONLINE LEARNING
OPERATIONALIZATION FROM STREAMING DATA
Rolling out models that learn and serve predictions based
on high-velocity data streams is a challenging problem that
presents difficulties in a number of areas.
A. Learning and serving predictions in the context of Big Data
When training data is supplied in a high-velocity fashion,
the model training phase must be sufficiently quick so that
no data gets discarded. Moreover, storing large amounts of
data and applying batch training is often prohibitive from
a cost perspective (storage) and could also be unfeasible
because the storing/re-training overhead may introduce a lag
that propagates through the pipeline and compounds with each
storage/re-training iteration. Online learning can alleviate the
issues related to storing and training using mini-batches, but
the model updates still need to be performed swiftly enough
so that the updating phase does not get overwhelmed by the

issues related to storing and training using mini-batches, but
the model updates still need to be performed swiftly enough
so that the updating phase does not get overwhelmed by the
incoming high-velocity observations. Whatever an architecture
is designed to tackle these challenges, it needs to factor in the
following:
1) The architecture should be able to address the challenges
for both the training and model serving phases of the
pipeline
2) The architecture should be elastically scalable so that it
can be quickly adapted to changes in the predictions

B. Requirements for operationalization of machine learning
models in the banking industry
Here we expand on item 6 above. Using machine learning
models in a highly regulated industry such as Finance comes
with an additional set of requirements and safeguards that must
be put around such critical systems.
Reproducibility of predictions and model This is required
for the purposes of auditability. Every prediction generated by
the model at time X must be fully reproducible. There should
be a mechanism in place, which preserves model versions, so
model instances can be resurrected as of time X and used for
reproducible predictions that were generated in the past.
Consistency of prediction Given the same input features,
the model should always produce the same outputs. This is

reproducible predictions that were generated in the past.
Consistency of prediction Given the same input features,
the model should always produce the same outputs. This is
challenging, especially if identical requests for scoring land
on different model-serving instances.
Auditability / Transparency : Model predictions must be
explainable. Moreover, there must be a mechanism in place
that, when enabled, can log requests and model responses on
demand.
Monitoring and roll-back to the previous model in produc-
tion Models, especially in the case when they are being con-
tinuously updated, should be constantly monitored for model
performance and concept drift. There should be mechanisms
in place that allow for a streamlined switch back to a previous
version if performance degradation is ever detected.

D. Case-Study: Online detection of malicious events from
evolving data streams
A typical type of model that is used in the banking industry
is “Anomaly Detection”. Two common examples are Credit
Card Fraud and Log-based IT Security Analysis. Such datasets
are usually highly dimensional. For our experiments, we chose
the Malicious URL [54] use-case because it has over 3 million
features, and each record has a small subset of any of these
features (similar to logs analysis). High-dimensional models
are memory intensive and take a long time to train. We
chose this challenging dataset because it is representative
of challenges one would encounter in a real-world banking
industry use case. Originally, [54] explored online learning
approaches for detecting malicious Web sites (those involved

of challenges one would encounter in a real-world banking
industry use case. Originally, [54] explored online learning
approaches for detecting malicious Web sites (those involved
in criminal scams) using lexical and host-based features of
the associated URLs. The authors show that this application
is particularly appropriate for online algorithms as the size
of the training data is larger than what can be efficiently
processed in batch. Moreover, the distribution of features that
typify malicious URLs is continuously changing.

FROM STREAMING DATA (STREAM MLO PS)
In this section, we present our proposed architecture, which
enables a production-grade operationalization for training and
serving predictions from the online learning models.

The following open-source tools are fundamental building

cessing engine for stateful computations over unbounded and

Online learning refers to training the model as the ground

architecture which uses Online Learning for Malicious URL

incoming URLs and make a binary classification regarding
whether a URL is malicious or not.

Because a system based on Kafka is fault-tolerant and a
consumer operator instance can fail and restart on another
node, it is an excellent design to periodically store each
consumer’s model indexed by a consumer identifier. In case
of failure, the recovered consumer operator can start using the
last saved version of the model.

For simplistic features where the entire feature set can be

feature Engineering must operate on complex data events
windows. Windowing of events can be temporal (Window of
10 mins which rolls every 1 minute), count-based (Window
of 10 events which rolls every event) or something more
complex and custom-built. Apache Flink is a mature open-
source streaming platform which supports rich windowing
semantics ideal for feature engineering pipelines deployed at
massive scale and event velocity. The streaming and feature
engineering pipeline responsible for windowing capabilities is
usually Java-based because it needs to be high-performance.

C. StreamMLops: Architecture for Practical Online Learn-
ing Audit & Reproducibility in industry
For some industrial and banking use-case, the OnlineM-
LOps architecture proposed to deploy the model in production
needs to be extended with additional requirements. For a use-
case such as malicious URL detection, this design is adequate.
However, when online learning is used for the banking sec-

needs to be extended with additional requirements. For a use-
case such as malicious URL detection, this design is adequate.
However, when online learning is used for the banking sec-
tor in highly regulated industries, for fraud detection, credit
risk scoring and any automated decision making, the design
must handle the following non-functional requirements such
as reproducibility of prediction and model, consistency of
prediction and explainability for audit purpose. To address
these requirements, we present StreamMLOps in Figure 4
modified design for the more generalized supervised learning
model type. The steps of our architecture during the model
serving life-cycle are the following:
1) The data scientist works with a static dataset to cre-
ate version 0 of the model and store it in the Model
Registry. Also, the metrics and the link to the dataset
used for model training are included for model version
reproducibility.
2) The streaming consumer consuming features from the

Registry. Also, the metrics and the link to the dataset
used for model training are included for model version
reproducibility.
2) The streaming consumer consuming features from the
topic of the feature will download version 0.
3) The feature topic will stream features consumed by the
model, and the predictions are published to the Inference
Topic.
4) The Predictions are saved to a Key/Value store (ex., an
Object Store like S3 or a database like Redis)
5) When the Label arrives, it can be added to the Key/Value
Store alongside the predictions.
6) The label’s arrival will trigger a message containing the
prediction, features and the label. The current version of
the model is incrementally trained, and the new model
version is published in the model registry. Again, the
revised model metrics and the incremental dataset (or a
link to it) are stored in the model registry to enable model
version reproducibility.
7) A message is placed on the control tropic indicating that

link to it) are stored in the model registry to enable model
version reproducibility.
7) A message is placed on the control tropic indicating that
a new model version is available.
8) The Prediction/Inference consumers will fetch the latest
version of the model and continue making inferences.
The pipeline does not pause when the model version is
refreshed. Each inference also contains the model version
number it was produced with for auditability.

b) River: Online machine learning in Python :
River [18] is an open-source online machine-learning library
written in Python. The main focus of River is instance-
incremental learning, meaning that every component (estima-
tors, transformers, performance metrics, etc.) is designed to be
updated one sample at a time 10. This is in alignment with the
streaming setting, where data is continuously generated, and
once seen, it is no longer available in further iterations (no

updated one sample at a time 10. This is in alignment with the
streaming setting, where data is continuously generated, and
once seen, it is no longer available in further iterations (no
storage). The infrastructure for real-world applications is often
difficult to change; accordingly, River is designed to be used on
multiple architectures. It is not intended as a stand-alone tool;
on the contrary, River is designed to integrate with other tools
available in the Python ecosystem and in real-world systems.
Another important design principle is that River is intended for
researchers and practitioners. Complete learning experiments
can be easily designed and implemented in a couple of lines
of code. On the other hand, power users can easily extend
and modify existing tools adapting them to their own needs.
We choose River as the stream learning library in our system
based on the above-mentioned qualities: flexibility to adapt
to existing architectures and its potential to serve two kinds

based on the above-mentioned qualities: flexibility to adapt
to existing architectures and its potential to serve two kinds
of users: data scientists and data engineers. In our proposed
architecture, data is handled by the pipeline and passed to the

• Naive Bayes (NB) [1] – A probabilistic classifier based
on applying Bayes’ theorem with strong independence
assumptions between the features.
• Hoeffding Adaptive Tree (HAT) [60]. Builds a decision
tree incrementally (Hoeffding Tree) and if concept drift
is detected, the model starts creating alternative branches.
If an alternate branch performs better in new data, then
it replaces the original branch in the tree.
We select these methods to showcase the seamless deploy-
ment of machine learning models in the proposed architecture.

VI. LESSONS LEARNED AND MLO PS BEST PRACTICES
This paper explored several best practices for Online Learn-
ing on a high-velocity feature stream. We explored the best
practices for Online Model Learning, Model Serving, the role
of Model Registry, MLOps processes to deploy models on
a live stream without requiring pipeline restarts, and, most
importantly, handling the practical challenge of Online Learn-
ing when the ground truth arrives at a later time. Next, we
share some lessons learned and best practices for production
(MLOps).
A. Feature Engineering with Apache Flink
In the discussion above, we have assumed that engineered
features arrive at a topic which the inference consumer uses

(MLOps).
A. Feature Engineering with Apache Flink
In the discussion above, we have assumed that engineered
features arrive at a topic which the inference consumer uses
to produce inferences. However, in a streaming architecture,
feature engineering presents significant challenges.
For example, if one of the features is the MAX value within
all values received within the last 30-minute window, which
rolls every minute in a high-velocity stream. Given an us-
case where we aim to detect credit card fraud, a feature could
be the geodetic distance between two consecutive transaction
locations for the last 30 transactions. It is impractical to save
these features to an online feature store and retrieve them when
making predictions. While extremely simple conceptually,
such a system would be prohibitively expensive due to the
large amount of horizontally scaled components needed to
work under heavy load.
For an unsupervised learning or fraud use case, predictions

such a system would be prohibitively expensive due to the
large amount of horizontally scaled components needed to
work under heavy load.
For an unsupervised learning or fraud use case, predictions
must be made on each transaction. The features have to be
constantly generated on windows of events which are stateful.
Apache Flink provides an excellent system for such use cases.
It has rich windowing and trigger semantics. The events can
be stored reliably in the operator’s local state (speed and
low latency, and high throughput), its checkpointing capability
allows it to recover from failures without losing window
states, and its save point capabilities allow modifications
and redeployment of the feature engineering pipeline without
losing state. The streaming feature engineering and inference
pipeline must be backed by a feature and model metadata store
to enable a horizontally scalable system capable of live feature
onboarding.
B. Architecture deployment in industry for production

pipeline must be backed by a feature and model metadata store
to enable a horizontally scalable system capable of live feature
onboarding.
B. Architecture deployment in industry for production
a) Model serving : We are using model serving over
the stream in our experimentation. We have a python based
Kafka consumer which processes the features as they arrive
and makes a prediction. The model is just a python program
which can be served in any manner needed by the use case.
And even multiple ways, such as on a stream and a model
endpoint.
b) Automating Model updates and Auto-scaling : Auto-
scaling can be done with Kubernetes. Model updates are
propagated via a Model Registry. We propose MLflow, but
virtually any model registry would work fine.
c) Architecture deployment on Cloud : We have used
open-source systems for model training and deployment.
These systems can easily be deployed to the cloud using
Infrastructure or Software as a Service.

open-source systems for model training and deployment.
These systems can easily be deployed to the cloud using
Infrastructure or Software as a Service.
d) Dealing with supervised online learning and Delayed
labelling: In the real world, the ground truth only arrives
later. Therefore the architecture must be capable of storing
the predictions and the feature set using a business key (Ex.
Credit Card Number and Transaction Id) to enable Online
Learning when the truth arrives. We have described this in
our architecture diagram for production application in Figure
4.
C. Dealing with real-time applications using Kafka
Because listening from a Kafka Topic is an I/O intensive
process, it is good to have each Model Serving Instance listen
to (and write to) fewer partitions of the source and sink
topic. At most, there can be as many parallel instances as
the number of Kafka partitions on a source topic. Throughput
is maximized if the number of partitions on the source and

topic. At most, there can be as many parallel instances as
the number of Kafka partitions on a source topic. Throughput
is maximized if the number of partitions on the source and
sink topics is identical. As the ratio of partitions to parallel
Model Serving Instances drops (increasing parallelism), the

throughput increases (and latency drops). We published thou-
sands of records together to the Kafka topic for each published
operation. Increasing the number may improve throughput but
at the slight cost of increased latency. Using a too-high value,
however, may exhaust memory. Ultimately the selection of
this number is a trade-off between throughput and memory
consumption because the messages are buffered in the memory
of the Model Serving Operator before they are flushed to
the inference topic. The best selection will depend on the
latency, the payload size of the inference message, and the
memory available on the machine hosting the Model Inference
operator. In this study, uncompressed messages were published
to the source and inference topics. Because this architecture
is I/O intensive (as opposed to CPU intensive), the use of
compression may increase throughput without hurting other
performance metrics. It is recommended to test the impact of

is I/O intensive (as opposed to CPU intensive), the use of
compression may increase throughput without hurting other
performance metrics. It is recommended to test the impact of
message compression for production use cases.
D. Architecture discussion
The benefit of the architecture is that it is open architecture.
The various components can be replaced with similar platform
components. Horizontally scalable systems can become expen-
sive. In this paper, we used plain JSON for sending messages
to Kafka. JSON is bulkier than binary formats like A VRO.
We should use binary payloads instead of text-based payloads
to make our system more cost-efficient. This will allow us to
meet the throughput requirements with less hardware.

leverage all data instead of depending on sampling for model
training. The proposed system supports high throughput and
latency. The system also supports constant relearning of the
model without compromising the important requirements of
auditability and reproducibility. Banking systems must not
only be fast but must also be responsible because the cost
of incorrect predictions can be high. Our system emphasizes
those features.
Code source We release all the materials (code and data)
in GitHub repository13 to reproduce experiments and speed up
the deployment of architectures design in Fig. 4 and Fig. 5 by
the research community and data practitioners.

CONCLUSION
This paper deals with the MLOps aspect of deploying
dynamic online machine-learning models for streaming data.
First, we provide key industrial challenges of online learn-
ing and the main blockers to their adoption in the banking
sector. Second, we propose a reference architecture that al-
lows continual learning and serving in a way that satisfies
the imposed production constraints. Experiments on a high-
dimensional dataset with millions of features prove that online
learning methods, ensemble-based and adaptive approaches
are efficient (in terms of time, space and accuracy) to deal
with feature-evolving data streams. We demonstrate that the
proposed system can successfully ingest and process high-
velocity streaming data and that online learning models can

with feature-evolving data streams. We demonstrate that the
proposed system can successfully ingest and process high-
velocity streaming data and that online learning models can
be horizontally scaled for practical purposes. StreamMLOps
design provides a seamless bridge between data science and
data engineering in a way that satisfies the imposed production
constraints. We also discuss critical issues around training
and scoring, reproducibility, and traceability to deal with
production and banking requirements.
We hope the insights shared here will encourage additional
development of AI models that can be easily deployed and
maintained in production. A hybrid research approach where
researchers, data scientists, and engineers / MLOps collaborate
within the same team should speed up production-oriented
delivery for complex applications scenarios. Our work and
resources can be used by industry practitioners to deploy
online learning models, which are currently released, and

delivery for complex applications scenarios. Our work and
resources can be used by industry practitioners to deploy
online learning models, which are currently released, and
dynamic and adaptive AI models to deliver real-time busi-
ness applications. Researchers can use our work to develop
and benchmark new or improved models to support the use
cases for applying self-adaptive and automated learning and
online models on a high-velocity stream with high throughput
requirements. Future research could address some of the
industrial challenges discussed in StreamAI [7], including
continuously serving & maintaining self-learning AI models
in production while dealing with constantly evolving laws
and regulations constraints regarding data privacy, audit and
automated decision (ethics in AI, Cloud act, GDPR 2, etc..).

ABSTRACT  
Metal-based Additive Manufacturing (AM) can realize fully dense metallic components and thus offers an op -
portunity to compete with conventional manufacturing based on the unique merits possible through layer-by- 
layer processing. Unsurprisingly, Machine Learning (ML) applications in AM technologies have been increas -
ingly growing in the past several years. The trend is driven by the ability of data-driven techniques to support a 
range of AM concerns, including in-process monitoring and predictions. However, despite numerous ML appli -
cations being reported for different AM concerns, no framework exists to systematically manage these ML models

cations being reported for different AM concerns, no framework exists to systematically manage these ML models 
for AM operations in the industry. Moreover, no guidance exists on fundamental requirements to realize such a 
cross-disciplinary platform. Working with experts in ML and AM, this work identifies the fundamental re -
quirements to realize a Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) platform to support process-based ML models for 
industrial metal AM (MAM). Project-level activities are identified in terms of functional roles, processes, systems, 
operations, and interfaces. These components are discussed in detail and are linked with their respective re -
quirements. In this regard, peer-reviewed references to identified requirements are made available. The re -
quirements identified can help guide small and medium enterprises looking to implement ML solutions for AM in 
the industry. Challenges and opportunities for such a system are highlighted. The system can be expanded to

the industry. Challenges and opportunities for such a system are highlighted. The system can be expanded to 
include other lifecycle phases of metallic and non-metallic AM.   
1. Introduction 
Additive Manufacturing (AM) or three-dimensional (3D) printing is 
used to fabricate products from digital designs and has recently gained 
enormous attention from industry and academia ( Gibson et al., 2021a ). 
A multitude of benefits is encouraging researchers to develop AM 
technologies that can rival conventional manufacturing techniques at 
the industrial scale. Some notable advantages include tool elimination, 
material saving, the printing of complex designs, cost reduction, part 
consolidation, accelerated time-to-market, reduced storage with virtual 
inventory, and large-scale manufacturing of personalized products 
( Mehrpouya et al., 2019 ). AM technologies also enable specialized ap -
plications such as the printing of multifunctional or multi-material

( Mehrpouya et al., 2019 ). AM technologies also enable specialized ap -
plications such as the printing of multifunctional or multi-material 
designs ( Liu et al., 2021a ). As a result, AM features offer solutions to 
both existing and new challenges faced by manufacturing enterprises 
and have enormous economic potential ( Piller et al., 2015 ). 
While AM offers great promise, it faces challenges that lead to quality 
issues in the printed parts. For example, the lengthy nature of layer-wise 
material addition inevitably leads to different types of defects being 
introduced during the process of printing parts. The common techniques 
for plastic-based printing are also confronted with an array of chal -
lenges. Bubbles, overfills, scars, underfill, and warpage are some of the 
most common defects in the material extrusion process which is a type of 
polymer-based 3D printing ( Oleff et al., 2021 ). Metal AM (MAM), a

most common defects in the material extrusion process which is a type of 
polymer-based 3D printing ( Oleff et al., 2021 ). Metal AM (MAM), a 
subcategory of AM that can generate fully dense metallic parts, faces 
defects such as pores, cracks, lack of fusion, inclusions, irregularly
